segments.p2-10-g774: А here introduces a contrast or continuation ('and / while / but'). Позднее is an adverb meaning 'later.' В некоторых направлениях uses в + prepositional plural (направлениях) to mean 'in certain directions/strands', and экзистенциализма is a genitive that specifies those directions as 'of existentialism' ('in certain strands of existentialism').
segments.p2-10-g846: This is an abbreviated citation marker: the single-letter "С." (lowercase "с.") is commonly used in Russian references to mean "page(s)." It functions as a bibliographic abbreviation and has no grammatical inflection here.
segments.p2-10-g847: This is a page-range following the citation marker, written as numerals with a hyphen and a closing parenthesis; it completes the bibliographic reference. There are no lexical or grammatical changes inside this token beyond punctuation.
segments.p2-10-g851: The phrase begins with the preposition variant "со" (a euphonic form of "с") taking the instrumental plural to mark accompaniment or association. "всевозможными" is an instrumental plural adjective meaning "all kinds of/every possible," modifying the following proper name in instrument case, "Вагенгеймами." "перестанут" is the 3rd-person-plural future perfective of "перестать" meaning "will stop/cease," followed by the infinitive "болеть" (here "to ache/hurt"). "ваши зубы" is the logical subject in nominative plural, placed after the verb for topical or emphatic word order (Russian permits this inversion), so the whole means "your teeth will cease to ache (even) with all sorts of Wagenheims."
segments.p2-10-g854: "Речь идет о" is an impersonal idiom meaning "it is about" or "the matter concerns." "зубных врачах" is the noun phrase in the prepositional plural ("about dental doctors/dentists"), and "Вагенгеймах" is a proper name in the corresponding locative/prepositional plural used in apposition to specify which dentists. The parenthetical abbreviation "см." stands for "see" (cf.) and signals a cross-reference; the opening parenthesis is not closed within this segment.
segments.p2-10-g817: This segment begins with a quoted French phrase and its Russian rendering in quotes; the dash frames the quotation as an appositive. «здесь» ('here') is an adverb pointing to the current passage and modifies the past tense verb «использовал» ('used'). «использовал» is masculine past tense (he used); «сжатое» is a neuter adjective agreeing with the neuter noun «определение», and «личности» is in the genitive (definition of the personality/person) modifying «определение». The double given name «Жан-Жака» is in the genitive to mark possession of «Руссо».
segments.p2-10-g818: A relative clause introduced by the neuter relative pronoun «которое», which agrees with «определение» (neuter). «он» ('he') is masculine nominative and refers back to Rousseau in context; «дал» is simple past (he gave/stated). The preposition «в» takes the prepositional plural «Зимних заметках» (title) and the phrase «о летних впечатлениях» is the usual prepositional construction 'about summer impressions' forming the title of the work.
segments.p2-10-g819: «следуя» is a деепричастие (adverbial participle / gerund) meaning 'following' or 'in the wake of'; it provides an adverbial, concessive or causal link to the main clause and requires a dative complement (supplied next). The comma marks the usual pause after an adverbial participle.
segments.p2-10-g820: «вероятно» is an adverb meaning 'probably' or 'apparently' and modifies the whole following adverbial phrase — it signals that what follows (following Heine's example) is the author's conjecture rather than a firm fact.
segments.p2-10-g821: This phrase is the dative complement required by «следуя»: «примеру» is dative singular of 'example' and «Гейне» is the proper name in dative ('to/after Heine'). There is no preposition; the dative case marks 'following the example of Heine.'
segments.p2-10-g822: «употребившему» is a past active participle in the dative singular masculine, agreeing with the dative noun «примеру» and thus forming the participial clause 'Heine, who used…'. The sequence «то же самое выражение» is an emphatic phrase meaning 'the very same expression/phrase' (то + же + самое intensifies sameness). «в отношении Руссо» is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with regard to Rousseau' (в отношении + genitive).
segments.p2-10-g823: «Уж как» is an idiomatic, colloquial intensifier (roughly 'just wait/you'll see how' or 'they'll really') that amplifies the verb. «докажут» is future tense, 3rd person plural ('they will prove'); «тебе» is the dative pronoun 'to you' marking the indirect object to whom the proof will be shown.
segments.p2-10-g824: «например» is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'for example' or 'for instance'; it is set off by commas and qualifies the following clause as just one possible example.
segments.p2-10-g825: «что» introduces a subordinate content clause ('that...'). «от» + genitive («обезьяны») means 'from' or 'of' in the sense of 'descended from'; «произошел» is past tense, masculine singular of 'произойти' used here as 'originated/descended' — the grammatical subject is omitted (elliptical) but understood (masculine), so the clause reads '(that [you]) descended from an ape.'
segments.p2-10-g828: This is a nominal statement (noun phrase used as a sentence) presenting the topic: «Интерес» in the nominative is the subject, «к вопросу» uses the dative to mean 'interest in/toward the question,' and «о происхождении человека» is a prepositional phrase 'about the origin of man.' Time is specified by «в начале 1864 г.» ('at the beginning of 1864'), where «г.» is the standard abbreviation for 'год' (year).
segments.p2-10-g829: Обострился is a past-tense, perfective, reflexive verb form (masculine singular), here agreeing with the previously mentioned subject (“interest”) and meaning “became more acute/intensified.” В связи с is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning “in connection with / owing to,” followed by an instrumental noun (выходом) that names the event causing the intensification; the instrumentals and prepositional phrases that follow (в Петербурге, в русском переводе) locate that event (place and form of publication). The final noun phrase is a genitive chain (книги [последователя [эволюционной теории]] Ч.)—“a book of a follower of the theory of evolution, Ch.” —where Ч. is an abbreviated initial for the author’s name.
segments.p2-10-g878: This is an initial written with a period: an abbreviated patronymic or middle initial (here standing alone as the segment "М."). In bibliographic or name notation in Russian, a single letter plus a dot represents an initial (e.g. М. = Михайлович); punctuation ties it to the neighbouring initial in the preceding context.
segments.p2-10-g879: "Достоевского" is the genitive singular of the name Достоевский and here marks authorship ("by Dostoevsky") as in a bibliographic citation; the quoted phrase that follows is the title. The preposition "О" (about/concerning) governs the prepositional plural, so "о новых литературных органах" and the repeated "о новых теориях" use the prepositional plural forms (новых + органах/теориях). The parentheses enclose the publication year.
segments.p2-10-g880: The dash functions as a written copula linking the foreign noun "Шенапан" (used as a name/label) to the predicate noun "негодяй" in the nominative: "Shenapan [is a] scoundrel." The parenthesis opens to indicate the original-language form; "франц." is an abbreviated adjective meaning "French" (shortened and punctuated), pointing to the French original given next.
segments.p2-10-g881: This is the French word "chenapan" given in Latin letters inside the parenthesis as the original term; it is uninflected here and closes the parenthetical remark. It simply reproduces the foreign lemma cited as the source of the Russian rendering.
segments.p2-10-g830: This segment names the author in apposition to the preceding phrase (последователя эволюционной теории Ч.). Дарвина is genitive (Darwin's), and Томаса Генри Гексли is the full personal name in the same (genitive) relation; the dates in parentheses give birth–death years. The quoted title О положении человека в ряду органических существ is the work's title; положение (noun) is in the nominative used within the title, человека is genitive meaning 'of man', в ряду is the preposition + noun 'in the series/line', and органических существ is a genitive attribute 'of organic beings.'
segments.p2-10-g831: Не исключена возможность is an impersonal/factual turn: возможность (feminine) is the subject, and исключена is a short-form past passive/adjectival participle agreeing with возможность ('is excluded/ruled out'); with Не it yields 'the possibility is not excluded' / 'it cannot be ruled out.'
segments.p2-10-g832: This is a that-clause introduced by что. Эта фраза героя = 'this phrase of the hero' (фраза is the noun, героя is genitive 'of the hero'). Является + instrumental (откликом) expresses 'serves as / is' — here 'is a response'; на governs the object of the response. Вызывавшие is a past active participle modifying статьи ('articles that provoked'), in agreement in number/case with статьи; В. is the anonymizing initial of the author of those articles.
segments.p2-10-g833: A single-letter segment: the capital A with a period is an anonymizing or abbreviated initial (here the initial of an author's name) standing alone as the attribution of the preceding noun ('В. А. Зайцева' split across sentences).
segments.p2-10-g834: Зайцева is the surname Zaitsev in the genitive singular, used to indicate authorship (as in 'статьи ... Зайцева' = 'articles by Zaitsev'); the comma shows continuation into a relative clause that follows.
segments.p2-10-g835: В которых introduces a relative clause ('in which' / 'in which [articles]') referring back to статьи; он is the subject 'he'. Вслед за is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'following' or 'in the wake of' and takes an object in the instrumental or an initial like К. (here the anonymizing initial K.), so the clause reads 'in which he, following K., ...' — the verb or rest of the clause follows in the next text.
segments.p2-10-g855: 'с.' is an abbreviation (short for 'страница/страницы') used in citations to indicate a page number; here it functions as a bibliographic label equivalent to English 'p.' or 'pp.'. It is an independent minimal segment consisting of the single abbreviated form.
segments.p2-10-g856: This segment is the numeric page reference with closing parenthesis. Grammatically it's a cardinal numeral used as a citation (page 478) followed immediately by punctuation closing the parenthetical reference.
segments.p2-10-g857: 'Судя по ...' is an impersonal idiomatic construction meaning 'judging by' or 'according to'; 'Судя' is a gerund/participle form from 'судить' used impersonally ('judging'), and it governs the following prepositional phrase. 'По' here takes the dative ('Всеобщей адресной книге'), and 'Санкт-Петербурга' is the genitive that specifies the book's scope ('of Saint Petersburg'). The quoted title is therefore in the dative because of 'по'.
segments.p2-10-g858: в середине 1860-х годов — 'in the middle of the 1860s': 'в середине' + genitive plural time expression ('1860-х годов') marks a time frame. 'В Петербурге было восемь зубных врачей' is an existential construction: 'в Петербурге' indicates location; 'было' is the impersonal past tense 'there were'; the numeral 'восемь' governs the noun in genitive plural 'врачей', with the adjective 'зубных' agreeing in genitive plural. 'по фамилии Вагенгейм' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'with/by the surname Wagenheim' (here 'по' takes the dative 'фамилии').
segments.p2-10-g859: вывески — nominative plural 'signs' (shop signs/placards). Grammatically this is the subject of the following participial clause; it refers back to the advertising material mentioned.
segments.p2-10-g860: их рекламирующие — a participial attributive phrase: 'рекламирующие' is the present active participle (plural nominative) agreeing with 'вывески' and means 'advertising'; 'их' is the pronoun functioning inside the participle phrase as the object of 'рекламирующие' (i.e., 'advertising them' — advertising the dentists). Word order is the common attributive order where the pronoun precedes the participle.
segments.p2-10-g861: были распространены по всему городу — 'were spread/were widespread throughout the city': 'были' is the past plural auxiliary/finite verb 'were'; 'распространены' is the short-form past passive participle used predicatively (passive voice meaning 'were distributed' or 'were widespread'); 'по всему городу' is a prepositional phrase ('throughout the whole city'), where 'по' takes the dative 'всему городу'.
segments.p2-10-g865: Как here means “as” (introducing a role or comparison) and is followed by человек in the nominative, so this phrase functions as an appositive/characterization: “as a man.” The comma marks this as a brief introductory label for the following descriptive phrase.
segments.p2-10-g866: Отрешившийся is a past active participle (from the reflexive verb отрешиться) describing the person: “having detached himself/withdrawn.” It takes the preposition от + genitive (от почвы) meaning “from the soil/ground,” and the second genitive phrase народных начал (“of the people’s beginnings/principles”) completes the metaphorical object of detachment; the whole quoted clause is an adjectival description of the человек.
segments.p2-10-g867: Как теперь выражаются is a parenthetical comment meaning “as they now express it / as it is now said.” Выражаются is the 3rd-person plural present reflexive/passive form of выражать, here impersonal: the agents (“they/people/speakers”) are omitted but implied, so the phrase functions like the English idiom “as they now say/put it.”
segments.p2-10-g868: «Выражение» is a feminine noun in the nominative singular functioning as the sentence subject; the preceding dash is punctuation marking a remark or direct comment. It is the head noun modified by the following participial phrase.
segments.p2-10-g869: «взятое» is the short/attributive past passive participle (neuter nominative singular) of взять, agreeing with the neuter-sense subject «выражение»; «в» is the preposition and «кавычки» is its object (accusative plural) — together they form the phrase «взятое в кавычки» meaning 'taken/put into quotation marks.'
segments.p2-10-g870: «характерно» is a predicative (neuter form used impersonally) meaning 'is characteristic/typical'; «для статей журналов ...» is a prepositional phrase with «для» + genitive (статей, журналов) indicating what the expression is characteristic of.
segments.p2-10-g871: «Ср.» is an abbreviation (from «сравните» or «сравни») used as a bibliographic/reading note meaning 'compare' or 'cf.' and stands alone as an imperative or editorial marker.
segments.p2-10-g872: This segment is just the comma punctuation introducing a parenthetical or parenthetic continuation of the preceding note.
segments.p2-10-g873: «например» is an adverbial parenthetical meaning 'for example' used to introduce illustrative instances.
segments.p2-10-g874: «объявления» is nominative (or here part of an elliptical object list) meaning 'announcements'; «об» is the preposition meaning 'about/concerning' and takes the prepositional case; «издании» is a noun in the prepositional case here meaning 'publication' (об издании = concerning the publication) and «журнала» is in the genitive ('of the journal') specifying which publication.
segments.p2-10-g875: This segment continues the enumerated years (1862) and serves as a simple list item separated by commas.
segments.p2-10-g876: «1863 гг.» is the final item in the list of years; «гг.» is the standard abbreviation for 'years' (plural).
segments.p2-10-g877: «или» is the coordinating conjunction 'or' linking this alternative example to the previous ones; «статью» is the accusative singular of «статья» ('an article') and here names another item to compare; «Ф.» is an initial/abbreviation indicating the author (e.g., 'F.').
segments.p2-10-g882: Художник is a masculine noun in the nominative singular functioning as the sentence topic/subject; the comma after it sets it off as the thing being talked about (’the artist’).
segments.p2-10-g883: например is an adverb meaning ‘for example’ used parenthetically; commas mark it as an aside that qualifies the statement that follows.
segments.p2-10-g884: написал is the past tense, masculine singular, perfective form of написать (‘wrote/created’); картину is the direct object in the accusative singular (‘a painting’); Ге follows the object and here functions as the maker’s name in an indeclinable/abbreviated form indicating authorship (‘by Ge’). Together: ‘(he) wrote/painted a picture by Ge.’
segments.p2-10-g885: Автор is the nominative subject (‘the author’); написал is past tense, masculine singular of написать (‘wrote’/‘composed’); the quoted phrase как кому угодно is the direct object (what he wrote). In that frozen idiom как кому угодно, как = ‘as/like’, кому is an indefinite pronoun in the dative meaning ‘to whom/anyone’, and угодно is the short-form adjective meaning ‘pleasing’ — together ‘as anyone pleases/ however one likes.’
segments.p2-10-g888: Здесь is an adverb meaning ‘here’ used in the contextual sense (‘in this passage’); полемический выпад is a noun phrase with the adjective before the noun meaning ‘a polemical/verbal attack’; против is a preposition meaning ‘against’ that governs a following name in the genitive; М. is an abbreviated/initial form standing for a person (here given as M.). The clause is nominal (no copula) and reads ‘Here (is) a polemical attack against M.’
segments.p2-10-g889: Е. is a single-letter initial or abbreviation (an initial standing for a name); by itself it functions as a signature or an abbreviated reference to a person.
segments.p2-10-g896: A standalone year written as a numeral; here it functions as a dateline or date marker. The period is punctuation ending the sentence (not an abbreviation).
segments.p2-10-g898: A section or item number given as a numeral with a period; functions as a heading or ordinal label rather than a grammatical word. No inflection or case marking applies.
segments.p2-10-g899: A quoted noun phrase in the nominative (наша + adjective + noun) with closing quotation mark and closing parenthesis; word order is possessive pronoun + adjective + noun, all agreeing in gender, number, and case (feminine singular nominative). The punctuation (closing quote and parenthesis) indicates this phrase ends a quotation that was parenthetically inserted.
segments.p2-10-g900: Demonstrative pronoun + noun in feminine singular nominative (Эта картина) forming the sentence subject ('this painting'). The comma signals that a participial phrase will follow to modify this noun.
segments.p2-10-g901: A postpositive participial clause. 'показанная' is a past passive participle agreeing with 'картина' (feminine singular) meaning 'shown' (short for 'которая была показана'); 'впервые' is an adverb 'for the first time' modifying the participle. 'на осенней выставке' is a prepositional phrase ('at/on the autumn exhibition') where 'осенней' agrees with 'выставке'. 'Академии художеств' is a genitive attribute ('of the Academy of Arts'). Finally 'в 1863 г.' ('in 1863 yr.') gives the time; the preposition 'в' before the year marks temporal use.
segments.p2-10-g890: A hyphenated proper name (the writer Saltykov‑Shchedrin). It stands before a comma and is the noun that the following participial clause modifies; the form shown is the name as cited (its case will be determined by the role of the whole phrase in the larger sentence).
segments.p2-10-g891: "напечатавшего" is a past active participle (perfective) in the masculine singular, here agreeing with the preceding name and meaning "who printed/published"; because participles govern objects, "отзыв" is its direct object (accusative), and "сочувственный" is an adjective modifying "отзыв." The preposition "о" takes the prepositional case "картине" ("about the painting"), and "Н." is the abbreviated initial referring to the painter/owner of the painting.
segments.p2-10-g892: An abbreviated initial ("Н."), a conventional way in Russian to indicate a person's name only by its first letter; here it stands for the name of the painter mentioned in the previous phrase.
segments.p2-10-g893: An appositional note giving the artist's surname (Ge) followed by life dates in parentheses and the title of the work in quotes. Grammatically this is a parenthetical specification: the quoted title is in nominative as the name of the painting, and the parenthesis opens to give a bibliographic pointer (the clause is unfinished here).
segments.p2-10-g894: A colon used here to introduce the source or bibliographic reference that follows.
segments.p2-10-g895: "Современник" is the title of a periodical (Sovremennik); capitalized and used as a bibliographic reference (here in nominative as the name of the magazine).
segments.p2-10-g933: A simple comma punctuation mark; here it separates the preceding bibliographic element (the city abbreviation in the previous context) from the year. In Russian citations a comma commonly follows the city before the year.
segments.p2-10-g934: The four-digit year (1863) followed by a period that ends this short sentence/fragment; it names the publication year and links back to the city before the comma (i.e., ‘Saint Petersburg, 1863’). The period is sentence-final punctuation rather than an abbreviation mark.
segments.p2-10-g935: The abbreviation 'Т.' (capital letter plus period) stands for том (tom) = "volume" in bibliographic Russian. The period marks an abbreviation; this label will be read together with the following numeral to give the volume number.
segments.p2-10-g936: The Arabic numeral '1' with a period; in context this is the volume number (i.e. 'Volume 1'). The period is sentence- or item-final punctuation and here also follows the convention for numbering in citations.
segments.p2-10-g937: The abbreviation 'С.' (capital plus period) stands for страница/страницы (stranitsa/stranitsy) = "page(s)" in bibliographic Russian. It introduces the page range that appears in the following context; the period marks the abbreviation.
segments.p2-10-g947: В here introduces a time interval and is used with years to mean 'during / in the period of'. The numeral range 1861–1865 with the abbreviation гг. (годы) marks a plural year-span; the whole phrase is an adverbial time expression standing alone.
segments.p2-10-g948: в Северной Америке is a locative adverbial phrase ('in North America'). шла война uses the imperfective past verb шла (from идти) in the sense 'was going on / was being waged' — a common impersonal way to say 'a war was taking place'. против introduces the object of the war ('against ...') and is followed by a participial phrase: поднявших мятеж modifies штатов and is a past active participle meaning 'who had raised/started a revolt'; рабовладельческих is the attributive adjective 'slaveholding' modifying штатов, and Юга is a genitive specifying 'of the South'.
segments.p2-10-g949: сопротивлявшихся is a plural past participle (agreeing with штатов) from the imperfective сопротивляться and here means 'who were resisting'. отмене рабства is a dative construction (сопротивляться takes the dative) meaning 'the abolition of slavery'—отмене (dative of отмена) = 'to the abolition', рабства = 'of slavery'.
segments.p2-10-g950: Вот вам is a formulaic way to present or point something out to the listener: вот = 'here/there is', вам = dative 'to you' (polite/colloquial). The phrase functions to draw attention to what follows.
segments.p2-10-g951: наконец is an adverb meaning 'finally' or 'at last' and serves as a comment on timing or culmination; it modifies the whole utterance and is often set off by commas.
segments.p2-10-g952: карикатурный is an attributive adjective 'caricatured / grotesque' modifying the following proper name. Шлезвиг-Гольдштейн is a hyphenated place-name (Schleswig–Holstein); the adjective and the name together form a noun phrase in the nominative used as the thing being presented.
segments.p2-10-g955: This is a noun phrase functioning as an appositive or brief identification: Немецкое (neuter adjective) agrees with герцогство (neuter noun) to mean 'the German duchy'. The place-name Шлезвиг-Гольштиния follows to specify which duchy, and с 1773 г. is a temporal prepositional phrase ('since 1773') where с + year (аббр. г.) indicates the starting point.
segments.p2-10-g927: В + prepositional (в двухтомном труде) marks location: 'in the two-volume work'. Двухтомном is an adjective in the prepositional singular agreeing with труде (труд in the sense of a written work/product). Английского историка и социолога Генри Томаса Бокля is a chain of genitives modifying труде ("of the English historian and sociologist Henry Thomas Buckle"). The quoted title follows as an appositive naming the work. Высказана мысль uses a short passive participle (высказана) with the noun мысль (feminine) to mean "the thought/idea was expressed" (a common impersonal/passive construction in Russian).
segments.p2-10-g928: Что introduces a subordinate (that-) clause serving as the content of мысль: 'that ...'. Развитие цивилизации is the nominative subject ('the development of civilization'). Ведет is present tense, 3rd person singular of вести used in the sense 'leads to' (вести к + noun). К + noun in dative (к прекращению) marks direction/result 'to/ towards the cessation'. Войн is genitive plural 'of wars'; между народами is a prepositional phrase meaning 'between peoples/nations' (народами here denotes nations/peoples). См. is the bibliographic abbreviation 'see'.
segments.p2-10-g929: A colon marking the start of the bibliographic/citation label that follows; functions as punctuation only.
segments.p2-10-g930: Бокль here is the author's surname given as a citation head; in the citation the name appears in the nominative/heading form. Т. is an abbreviation (old printed form) for том/‘volume’ (i.e. vol.). Together they form a brief bibliographic reference (author + volume).
segments.p2-10-g931: A book title in Russian: История (nominative) + цивилизации (genitive) = 'History of civilization'; в Англии uses в + prepositional to mean 'in England'. As a title it is a standalone nominative phrase naming the work.
segments.p2-10-g932: СПб. is the common abbreviated form for Санкт-Петербург (Saint Petersburg) and here indicates place of publication in the bibliographic line.
segments.p2-10-g995: Abbreviated heading: 'примеч.' is a short form of 'примечание' ('note' or 'annotation') marked with a period as an abbreviation and stands alone as the label for a remark or footnote.
segments.p2-10-g996: 'к' is the preposition meaning 'to/toward' and here introduces the target of the note; it normally governs a dative form. 'с.' is an abbreviation for 'страница' / 'страницa' ('page') used in references, so 'к с.' begins the phrase 'to page ...'.
segments.p2-10-g997: A numeral giving the page number being referenced; here it completes the reference begun by 'к с.' (i.e. 'to page 416').
segments.p2-10-g998: 'Полемический' is an adjective in the nominative meaning 'polemical' and modifies 'намек' (also nominative). 'Намек' ('hint') takes the preposition 'на' plus its complement (here the quoted title) — with 'на' introducing the object/target of the hint. The final phrase 'в романе Н.' is an adverbial locative (in the novel by N.), where 'Н.' is an abbreviation indicating the author's name/initial.
segments.p2-10-g913: The quoted phrase is the title of the article; the dash links this title appositionally to the noun 'статья' (i.e. '“title” — an article'). 'Как' here functions as 'as/like' in the idiomatic title, 'кому' is dative meaning 'to whom/anyone', and 'угодно' is the short-form adjective meaning 'pleasing' (so together 'as anyone pleases'). 'Щедрина' is the author's surname in the genitive, marking possession ('article of Shchedrin').
segments.p2-10-g914: 'Напечатанная' is a past passive participle (full adjective form) agreeing with 'статья' and forming a post-nominal participial phrase modifying it; it indicates that the article was printed/published. 'в "Современнике"' names the periodical (locative use of 'в'), and 'за 1863 г.' uses 'за' with a time expression to mean 'for/in the year 1863' (abbreviated 'г.').
segments.p2-10-g916: A parenthetical or footnote marker (the number 7 followed by a closing parenthesis); it has no lexical content to analyze here.
segments.p2-10-g917: The dash links two nominal phrases in apposition: 'Сандальный нос' names a type and the second phrase defines it as 'нос пьяницы' ('the nose of a drunkard'). 'Сандальный' is an adjective meaning 'sandal-related/sandal-like', the first 'нос' is the head noun 'nose', and 'пьяницы' is the genitive singular of 'пьяница', showing possession/characteristic ('of a drunkard').
segments.p2-10-g921: 'Ну' is a pragmatic filler/interjection often translated 'well'; 'хоть' here is a particle meaning 'at least' or 'even', softening the claim; 'утверждать' is the imperfective infinitive 'to assert' used to propose a possible contention (impersonal suggestion: 'one could assert').
segments.p2-10-g922: 'Например' is an adverbial parenthetical meaning 'for example' that introduces an illustrative case.
segments.p2-10-g923: 'Вслед за' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'following' or 'in the footsteps of' and takes the instrumental; 'Боклем' is the instrumental form of the name (Buckle) and therefore the person being followed. The provided lemma senses list does not include this proper-name reading, so the name itself is treated as outside those senses.
segments.p2-10-g924: 'Что' is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the content clause. 'От' here means 'from'/'because of' (indicating source or cause), 'цивилизации' is the genitive 'of civilization', 'человек' is the generic nominative subject 'man/people', and 'смягчается' is the present passive/reflexive verb 'becomes softened' (3rd person singular), yielding 'that civilization softens man' or 'man is softened by civilization.'
segments.p2-10-g973: Клеопатра is a proper noun in the nominative (the subject/name) and here names the entry. The parenthetical 69-30 shows a date range; до is the preposition meaning ‘before’ (used in the standard Russian abbreviation до н. э. = ‘before our era’/BC) and н. is the first letter of the standard abbreviation that continues across the segment break. The dash between the numbers indicates a lifespan range (69–30).
segments.p2-10-g974: Э. here completes the abbreviation н. э. (нашей эры) meaning ‘of our era’ — conventionally rendered BC when paired with до. The closing parenthesis and period are part of that abbreviation and punctuation; this is an orthographic/abbreviated element rather than a lexical interjection.
segments.p2-10-g975: The dash introduces an appositive definition: царица Египта is a noun phrase with царица (nominative) plus the genitive Египта ‘of Egypt’ (queen of Egypt). Из + genitive (из династии) means ‘from/of (the) dynasty’, and Птоломеев is the genitive plural naming the Ptolemaic family.
segments.p2-10-g976: Имя ее literally places the noun before the possessive (имя = ‘name’, её = ‘her’); this order is stylistic but equivalent to её имя. Часто упоминалось uses the impersonal/passive reflexive verb упоминаться in the past tense (neuter singular to agree with имя), meaning ‘was often mentioned’. В русской печати is a prepositional phrase (‘in the Russian press’), where печати is the prepositional singular of печать used for ‘press/publications’. В 1861 г. gives the time ‘in 1861’, with г. as the standard abbreviation for год (‘year’).
segments.p2-10-g977: см. is the standard abbreviation of смотреть/смотри or см. used in references meaning ‘see’ or ‘cf.’; it opens a cross-reference or parenthetical pointer. The punctuation shows the parenthesis remains open here.
segments.p2-10-g956: Фактически is an adverb meaning 'in fact/de facto' that modifies the verb; стало is the past tense, neuter singular form of стать ('to become') agreeing with the implied neuter subject (the duchy) and expresses a change of state; провинцией is the predicate noun in the instrumental case (стать + Instrumental = 'become X'); Дании is the genitive that completes 'провинцией Дании' = 'a province of Denmark'.
segments.p2-10-g957: В with a year is the temporal preposition 'in/during' (here used for time); the numeral 1864 names the year; г. is the standard abbreviation for год ('year'). The whole segment is a time adverbial.
segments.p2-10-g958: Шла is the past tense of идти used impersonally/intransitively to mean 'was proceeding' or 'was being waged' (common with война); word order (verb before noun) is a stylistic inversion: normally 'война шла', but 'шла война' emphasizes the ongoing action. Прусско-датская is a compound adjective (прусско- + датская) in the feminine nominative to agree with война; война is the feminine nominative subject 'war'.
segments.p2-10-g959: Закончившаяся is a past passive/qualifying participle (закончившийся) agreeing with война (fem.), functioning like a relative clause 'which ended'; присоединением is a noun in the instrumental case meaning 'by/with the annexation' (the Instrumental often names the means/result after закончиться); этой территории is a genitive attribute to присоединением ('the annexation of this territory'); к + Пруссии uses the preposition к with the dative to indicate direction/recipient — 'to Prussia' (i.e. annexed to Prussia).
segments.p2-10-g963: This is a terse nominal list. Атиллы is a pluralized proper name used generically ('Attilas' — Attila-type conquerors or Attila himself used as a type); да here is an archaic conjunction meaning 'and' (common in colloquial/older style); Стеньки Разины are the two-word name in the same stylistic form, referring to the historical rebel Stenka Razin. The whole phrase names archetypal violent figures.
segments.p2-10-g902: A comma that links this clause to the preceding sentence; it marks continuation and should be read as the same sentence begun earlier.
segments.p2-10-g903: Compound verb phrase: 'вызвала' is the past tense, feminine singular form of вызвать (here meaning 'evoke/bring about') and takes a direct object; 'разноречивые толки' is the accusative plural noun phrase (adjective + noun) meaning 'conflicting/mixed interpretations' or 'contradictory talk.' Word order is typical: verb + object, with the adjective preceding the noun.
segments.p2-10-g904: 'Впоследствии' is an adverb meaning 'afterward/ subsequently' placed at the clause start for temporal framing; 'Достоевский' is the subject; 'упрекал' is the past imperfective (indicative) form meaning 'reproached/criticized' (imperfective implying repeated or general action); 'Н.' is an abbreviated proper name (initial plus period) serving as the direct object. Word order is standard: adverb — subject — verb — object.
segments.p2-10-g905: A single orthographic initial with a period standing for a person’s name; used here as a nominal reference (the abbreviated name itself, functioning as a noun in whatever case the sentence requires).
segments.p2-10-g906: 'Ге' is the painter's surname (here the person held responsible) used as the name of the accused party; the phrase 'в преднамеренном смешении' is 'в' + noun in the prepositional case (преднамеренном смешении) and means 'in the deliberate mixing'; the quoted adjectives 'исторической и текущей' agree in case and modify 'действительности' (genitive), so the whole phrase reads 'Ge (for) in the deliberate mixing of "historical and current" reality.' In the larger sentence this phrase continues the complaint introduced earlier (i.e. 'he reproached Ge in/for ...').
segments.p2-10-g907: 'Отчего' here is a relative/correlative adverb meaning 'whereby/because of which' and introduces the consequence clause that follows; it links the preceding accusation to the result.
segments.p2-10-g908: 'По его мнению' is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in his opinion' or 'according to him.' Grammatically 'по' governs the dative ('его мнению'); the whole phrase qualifies the following quoted judgment as Dostoevsky's view.
segments.p2-10-g909: Start of the quotation. 'вышла' is the past tense, feminine singular form of выйти used impersonally here ('there came out/it turned out'); syntactically it agrees with the nearest feminine noun 'фальшь' though the full subject is the coordinated phrase 'фальшь и предвзятая идея'. The phrase states the alleged result: 'a falseness and a prejudiced idea came out.'
segments.p2-10-g910: Continuation of the quotation: 'а' marks contrast ('and/but'); 'всякая фальшь есть ложь' uses the present-tense copula 'есть' for a general truth ('every falseness is a lie'); 'и уже вовсе не реализм' is an emphatic negation where 'уже' has the sense 'no longer' and 'вовсе' strengthens the negation ('at all/by no means'), so together they mean 'and is in no way realism anymore.' The closing quotation mark ends Dostoevsky's remark and the parenthetical citation begins.
segments.p2-10-g911: A year in the citation, written in numerals and separated by commas to set off the bibliographic parenthesis.
segments.p2-10-g912: Parenthetical citation title: 'По поводу выставки' is a phrase meaning 'On the occasion of the exhibition' or 'Concerning the exhibition.' 'По поводу' is a prepositional combination meaning 'concerning/about' and 'выставки' is the genitive of 'выставка' ('exhibition'). The closing parenthesis ends the citation.
segments.p2-10-g966: The initial dash marks a list entry or apposition. Атилла is a proper name (Attila); the following opening parenthesis and question mark indicate an uncertain or unknown birth date (so the author writes the name followed by '(?' before the closing date).
segments.p2-10-g967: The closing parenthesis with "-453" gives the death year (died 453); the following dash (em dash) introduces the appositive description. "вождь" is a nominative noun meaning 'chief' or 'leader'; "племени" is the genitive of "племя" ('tribe'), and "гуннов" is the genitive plural of "гунн" ('the Huns'), so together 'leader of the Hun tribe.'
segments.p2-10-g968: "возглавлявший" is a past active participle (from возглавлять) agreeing with "вождь" and functioning as a relative clause meaning 'who led' (imperfective/habitual or descriptive past). "опустошительные" (plural) modifies "походы" and means 'devastating'; "военные" further specifies them as 'military'; "походы" is plural 'campaigns' or 'expeditions'. The prepositional phrase "на территории Римской империи" uses "на территории" + genitive to mean 'on/in the territory of the Roman Empire.'
segments.p2-10-g969: This is a simple coordinated genitive phrase listing additional regions as objects of the preceding prepositional phrase: "Ирана и Галлии" — 'of Iran and Gaul.' Both nouns are in the genitive to follow "на территории (чего)".
segments.p2-10-g970: This gives the full personal name with patronymic: "Степан" (given name), "Тимофеевич" (patronymic), "Разин" (surname). "ок." is the common abbreviation of "около" meaning 'approximately' (used before dates).
segments.p2-10-g971: The parenthetical years "1630-1671)" close the birth–death dates; the dash introduces the appositive. "донской" is an adjective 'of the Don' (region), and "казак" is the noun 'Cossack' in nominative, naming his social/ethnic identity.
segments.p2-10-g972: "возглавивший" is a past active participle (agreeing with "казак") meaning 'who led.' "крестьянскую" is the accusative feminine adjective 'peasant' modifying "войну" (accusative 'war'), so 'the peasant war.' "в России" is the locative/in-prepositional phrase 'in Russia.' The final dates "1667-1671 гг." specify the years of that war, with "гг." an abbreviation for 'years.'
segments.p2-10-g978: 'статью' is the accusative singular of статья (here 'article') and functions as the object of the omitted verb (cf. the preceding '(см.' = 'see'). The following name phrase supplies the author; the noun precedes its genitive author phrase here. 'Ф.' is an abbreviated initial that belongs to that author phrase.
segments.p2-10-g979: 'М.' is an initial (here the second initial of the author's name, e.g. 'M.' for Mikhailovich). It is written in abbreviated form with a period and is part of the genitive name phrase that names the author of the article; initials do not show case endings, the case is carried by the surname that follows.
segments.p2-10-g980: 'Достоевского' is the genitive singular of the surname and together with the initials forms the genitive author phrase modifying 'статью' (the article by Dostoevsky). The quoted title «Ответ ДРусскому вестнику» is a noun phrase: 'Ответ' ('Answer') is the title noun, and 'ДРусскому вестнику' functions inside the title as a dative phrase 'to the Russian Messenger' (here presented as part of the title). The final parenthetical '1861' gives the year of publication.
segments.p2-10-g984: 'он' is the nominative third-person pronoun 'he' (subject). 'сам' is an emphatic/reflexive particle emphasizing 'he himself.' 'не' is the negation 'not,' and 'более' here combines with 'не' in the fixed expression 'не более' meaning 'no more' or 'nothing more than.' Word order is subject + emphatic particle + negation + comparative particle.
segments.p2-10-g985: 'как' here is a comparative/linking particle 'as' or 'like.' 'нечто' is an indefinite pronoun 'something.' 'вроде' takes a noun in the genitive and means 'sort of'/'like'; together 'нечто вроде' = 'something like.' 'фортепьянной' is an adjective 'piano-' in the genitive singular modifying 'клавиши,' and 'клавиши' is genitive singular of 'клавиша' ('key'), governed by 'вроде.' The whole phrase yields 'something like a piano key.'
segments.p2-10-g938: This segment is a numeric page range used parenthetically; the double hyphen denotes an en-dash in printed text and the closing parenthesis ends the citation. It functions as a label, not a grammatical phrase.
segments.p2-10-g939: "Вот вам" is a deictic, idiomatic phrase meaning "here/there (for you)"; "Наполеон" is the nominative proper name; the dash introduces a correlative construction in which the first "и" begins an "и...и..." pair that links to the next segment, and "великий" is an adjective used predicatively ("the Great").
segments.p2-10-g940: The second "и" completes the correlative "и...и..." ("both...and..."); "теперешний" is an adjective used substantively/elliptically here ("the present one"), referring back to Napoleon and paralleling "великий."
segments.p2-10-g941: "Имена французских императоров" is the nominative subject phrase ("the names of the French emperors"); the dash sets off an appositive that specifies those emperors. The forms "Наполеона I ... и Наполеона III" are in the genitive because they complete the idea "names of (which) emperors"; "названы" is a short passive past participle/predicate meaning "are called/named" and "в связи с тем" is the idiom "in connection with the fact."
segments.p2-10-g942: "Что" introduces the explanatory subordinate clause. "Периоды правления" is the noun phrase "periods of rule/reign"; "того и другого" is a genitive correlative meaning "of the one and the other" (referring to the two Napoleons). "Ознаменовались" is a perfective reflexive verb meaning "were marked by," and "большим количеством войн" uses the instrumental (большим количеством) to indicate the means/measure "by a large number of wars," with "войн" in the genitive as the complement of количество.
segments.p2-10-g943: "Которые" is a plural relative pronoun referring back to "войн" (the wars) and here functions as the direct object of "вела." "Вела" is past tense, feminine singular, agreeing with the subject "Франция;" together "которые вела Франция" means "which France waged/conducted."
segments.p2-10-g944: "Вот вам" again is a deictic formula meaning "here/there (for you)." "Северная Америка" is a fixed proper name (adjective + noun) meaning the region/continent "North America" used as a noun phrase in the nominative.
segments.p2-10-g988: "Имеется в виду" is an impersonal/passive construction meaning "is meant" or "is referred to." "следующее рассуждение" is a nominal phrase "the following argument/reasoning" that functions as what is being meant; the following genitive phrase (французского философа-материалиста Дидро) modifies рассуждение ("the argument of the French materialist philosopher Diderot"). "в его сочинении ... (1769)" is a prepositional phrase indicating the source (in his work ...), with его as the possessive referring back to Дидро.
segments.p2-10-g989: Мы is the nominative plural pronoun "we." The dash stands for the copula "are" (a common Russian equative use). инструменты is the predicate nominative (plural) — a metaphorical noun "instruments/ tools." The clause is elliptical (no explicit verb) because the dash replaces "are."
segments.p2-10-g990: одаренные is a past passive participle (plural) agreeing with инструменты: "endowed" or "gifted with." It introduces a participial phrase describing the instruments. способностью uses the instrumental case to mark "with the capacity/ability" and is followed by the infinitive ощущать ("to feel/ perceive") which names the capacity; и памятью is a second coordinated instrumental noun "and (with) memory."
segments.p2-10-g991: Наши is the plural possessive "our," modifying чувства (nominative plural) "feelings/senses." The dash functions as the copula linking the subject to the predicate клавиши (nominative plural) "keys," yielding the equative meaning "Our feelings are keys."
segments.p2-10-g992: по которым is a relative phrase (по + relative pronoun) meaning "on/which" and refers back to клавиши. ударяет is 3rd person singular present "strikes" with the singular subject окружающая нас природа (окружающая is a present participle "surrounding" modifying природа; нас is the accusative object meaning "us"). The second relative clause introduced by которые (plural to match клавиши) has ударяют (3rd person plural) "strike" and the adverbial phrase часто сами по себе "often by themselves/of their own accord." Note the two relative clauses: nature strikes them, and they often strike on their own.
segments.p2-10-g993: Тогда is an adverb meaning "then" or "in that case." выстроится is the future tense, 3rd person singular of the reflexive verb выстроиться (used impersonally/passively) meaning "will be built/erected." The noun phrase хрустальный дворец (adjective + noun, nominative) is the logical subject; the verb-before-noun order is a stylistic inversion common in Russian.
segments.p2-10-g994: см. is an abbreviated imperative or editorial abbreviation (from смотреть/смотри or cf. 'see') used in references or notes, meaning "see" or "cf." It stands alone as a brief pointer.
segments.p2-10-g999: A single-letter capital plus a period: an initial standing for a person's surname (common 19th-century convention). Grammatically it functions as a nominal abbreviation on its own (no inflection or explicit grammatical role here).
segments.p2-10-g1000: "Чернышевского" is the genitive singular of the proper name Чернышевский, functioning as a possessive modifier of the title that follows; the quoted phrase is the title of the work (left in its original infinitive-question form). The whole segment names the work as belonging to or authored by Chernyshevsky.
segments.p2-10-g1002: "Там описано" uses the short/neuter past participle/impersonal passive описано with там to mean 'there is described' or 'is described there'; the participle is neuter because it agrees with the neuter noun здание that follows. "чугунно-хрустальное" is a hyphenated compound adjective (an adverbial form чугунно + adjective хрустальное) agreeing in gender, number and case with здание; "здание-дворец" is a compound noun ('building-palace') combining a generic noun and an appositive or emphatic noun.
segments.p2-10-g1003: "в котором" is the preposition в plus the relative pronoun который in the prepositional (locative) case, meaning 'in which' and introducing a relative clause describing the preceding noun (здание-дворец).
segments.p2-10-g1004: "как это представлялось" is an impersonal construction: как = 'as' / 'in the way that', это = 'this/it', and представлялось is the imperfective reflexive verb in the past (it presented itself / it was imagined / it seemed). The particle/word и before the initial "Ш." functions here as 'also' or 'even' ('even Sh.'), and "Ш." is an abbreviated initial for a surname.
segments.p2-10-g1005: Proper name in the nominative: "Фурье" refers to the thinker Fourier (here the social theorist Charles Fourier). The following abbreviation "см." (short for 'смотрите'/'see') is an editorial parenthetical 'see (cf.)', opening a parenthetical reference.
segments.p2-10-g1021: This is a single clause where the fronted noun phrase Колосс Родосский ('The Colossus of Rhodes') is left-dislocated and followed by a dash; the grammatical subject is actually г-н Анаевский (Mr. Anaevsky), who performs the verb свидетельствует (3rd person singular, present indicative, imperfective). The prepositional phrase о нем uses the pronoun нем (prepositional of он) to refer back to the Colossus; the dash signals a pause/apposition and an inversion where the thing referred to is stated before the speaker who testifies about it.
segments.p2-10-g1024: The dash at the start marks reported speech. Колосс Родосский is the nominative subject; the second dash links it to the appositive nominal phrase бронзовая статуя Гелиоса ('a bronze statue of Helios'). Бронзовая is a feminine nominative adjective modifying статуя; Гелиоса is genitive singular (Helios) indicating 'statue of Helios.'
segments.p2-10-g1025: This two-word phrase is an appositive clarification: бога is genitive singular of бог and солнца is genitive of солнце; together they mean 'the god of the sun' and modify/identify Гелиоса — i.e., Helios, the sun-god (both nouns in genitive forming a genitive attribute).
segments.p2-10-g1026: Высотою is an archaic/adverbial instrumental or old-style nominative form meaning 'with a height of'; it expresses measure and agrees with the implied noun статуя. The construction 'высотою в 32 м' literally means 'with a height of 32 m' — в introduces the numeric measure (в + accusative or prepositional numeric phrase), and м is the abbreviation for metres.
segments.p2-10-g1027: Изваянная is a feminine nominative past passive participle agreeing with статуя and means 'sculpted' or 'fashioned.' The preposition в plus '280 г.' gives the year when it was made; г. is the standard abbreviation for год ('year'). The full time expression is completed by the following abbreviated segments 'до н. э.' (before our era).
segments.p2-10-g1028: До is a preposition meaning 'before' in temporal phrases and governs the genitive; here it begins the standard abbreviated time phrase 'до н. э.' (до нашей эры, 'before our era' = B.C.). The letter 'н.' is an abbreviation element (stands for нашей) and is not used here as an independent lexical letter.
segments.p2-10-g1029: Э. is the final element of the conventional abbreviation 'до н. э.' and stands for эры (era). The period marks it as an abbreviation; together with the previous segment it yields 'до нашей эры' = 'before our era' (i.e., B.C.).
segments.p2-10-g1083: "Там же" is an adverbial phrase meaning "there (in the same place) / in that same passage," with "же" giving emphasis or continuity; "Гейне" is the nominative subject; "утверждал" is the past imperfective, 3rd person singular, meaning "asserted" and introduces reported speech (hence the following comma and subordinate clause).
segments.p2-10-g1084: "что" introduces the content clause ('that ...'); "Руссо" is the nominative agent of that clause; "в своей 'Исповеди'" is a prepositional phrase where the possessive "своей" reflexively refers to Rousseau and locates the remark in his book; "делает" is present tense (used here to report Rousseau's action or habit within the work); "лживые признания" is the direct object (accusative plural); "для того" begins a purpose construction which will be completed by "чтобы" in the next segment (i.e. the fixed sequence "для того, чтобы" = "in order to").
segments.p2-10-g1085: "чтобы скрыть" is the infinitival purpose clause (perfective infinitive "скрыть" = 'to hide') completing "для того" from the previous segment; "под ними" uses the preposition "под" + instrumental pronoun "ними" to mean "under them," where "them" refers back to "лживые признания"; "истинные поступки" is the object of "скрыть" (accusative plural, adjective agreeing in gender/number); "или из тщеславия" offers an alternative motive, with "из" governing the genitive "тщеславия" ("out of/for vanity").
segments.p2-10-g1086: "По поводу" is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning "regarding/on the subject of" and takes a noun in the genitive; "мокрого снега" is the genitive singular noun phrase (adjective + noun) agreeing in case, giving "wet snow." The whole is an elliptical, topical sentence used to mark the subject.
segments.p2-10-g1087: Here "Еще" means "more/another/further" (a brief interjection meaning 'one more' or 'further remark'); "П." is an initial or abbreviated marker (presented as a single letter with a period) — together this short line signals a further item or remark labelled "P." The dash indicates spoken or listed fragmentary speech.
segments.p2-10-g1088: A single initial "В." (with period) functioning as an initial or signature; read here as an abbreviated name or label (the punctuation shows it's an initial rather than the preposition "в").
segments.p2-10-g1101: Single-letter item used as an abbreviation or initial (here presented with a period). It stands alone as a graphic marker (likely an initial or editorial abbreviation) rather than a full word; punctuation shows it is an abbreviated form.
segments.p2-10-g1102: "Гончарова" is the genitive form of the author name Goncharov and marks authorship of the title that follows; the quoted title "Обыкновенная история" is the book title, and the year in parentheses is the publication year. The whole segment is an appositive noun phrase (author + title + date) set off by a comma from the following descriptive phrase.
segments.p2-10-g1103: A nominal predicate in apposition to the preceding title: "воплощение" (nominative) = 'embodiment,' followed by two genitives linked by "и" that specify what is embodied: "здравого смысла" ('of sound/sane sense') and "практической деловитости" ('of practical businesslike manner'). The adjective forms are in the appropriate case and agree with their nouns; the whole phrase functions as an explanatory descriptor of the book.
segments.p2-10-g1107: Compound predicative phrase: "свезут" is perfective future, 3rd person plural (literally 'they will transport/bring by vehicle'), with an implied agent 'they' and an implied object (context supplies who is taken). "в сумасшедший дом" uses "в" + accusative to indicate motion into the institution ('to the madhouse/insane asylum'). "в виде 'испанского короля'" is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the form/guise of' and takes the genitive "испанского короля" (both adjective and noun in genitive).
segments.p2-10-g1110: Clause with inverted word order: "Испанским королем" is in the instrumental case (used with "считать/считать себя" to indicate what someone is considered to be), followed by the past-tense verb "считал" (he considered) and the reflexive "себя" (himself). The subject "безумный Поприщин" follows the predicate (agreement: masculine past "считал"). The final prepositional phrase "в повести Гоголя 'Записки сумасшедшего' (1835)" locates the action in Gogol's novella (title in quotes) with the publication year in parentheses.
segments.p2-10-g1006: "его" is the invariable possessive pronoun 'his' modifying the quoted title; here it refers back to the named author in the preceding context. "Теорию" is the noun 'theory' in the quoted title, presented in the form (accusative/nominative shape inside a parenthetical 'see ...') and "всемирного единства" is a genitive noun phrase 'of universal unity' that post-modifies 'theory' (the noun of the title uses genitive to show the content/subject of the theory).
segments.p2-10-g1007: The numerals and closing parenthesis "1841)," are an appositive date giving the year (publication or composition) and close the parenthetical remark; grammatically they act as a parenthetical time-marker rather than a syntactic argument of the main clause.
segments.p2-10-g1008: "живут" is the third person plural present (here general/presentive) form of "жить" meaning 'live' or 'are living'; it is the main verb of the clause. "люди" is the plural subject 'people' (plural of человек). "социалистического общества" is a genitive attributive phrase 'of the socialist society' specifying which people are meant ('people of socialist society'); the genitive links the noun 'общества' to 'люди' as a descriptive/possessive phrase.
segments.p2-10-g1009: "Моделью" is the instrumental singular of "модель" and is used predicatively with "послужил" to mean 'served as a model' (instrumental case marks the role or capacity). "для" is the preposition 'for', which governs the genitive; it introduces "изображения" (a deverbal noun meaning 'the depiction' or 'to depict'), so "для изображения этого дворца" = 'for the depiction of this palace'. "этого дворца" is a genitive phrase 'of this palace' modifying "изображения". "послужил" is the past tense, masculine singular of "послужить" ('served as'), agreeing with the actual grammatical subject that follows. "хрустальный дворец" is the nominative subject (adjective + noun), and "в Лондоне" is a locative prepositional phrase 'in London' specifying where that Crystal Palace is located.
segments.p2-10-g1013: "прилетит" is the third-person singular future (perfective) form of "прилететь" meaning 'will arrive (by flying)'; the verb comes first in the Russian word order but in translation we render a normal English S-V order. "птица Каган" is the noun phrase subject ('the bird Kagan' or 'Kagan the bird')—a proper name following the common noun.
segments.p2-10-g1016: "по" plus the dative (here "народному преданию") forms the fixed idiom meaning 'according to' or 'by (popular) tradition'; both "народному" (dative adjective) and "преданию" (dative noun) are in the dative singular to match the government of "по" in this sense.
segments.p2-10-g1017: "эта" is the demonstrative pronoun 'this' in the nominative feminine singular modifying "птица". "легендарная" is the nominative feminine adjective 'legendary'. "птица" is the nominative subject 'bird'. "приносит" is present tense, third-person singular imperfective of "приносить" meaning 'brings' or 'yields' (habitual or general action); it takes a dative indirect object "людям" ('to people') and an accusative direct object "счастье" ('happiness'). The clause states a general belief expressed as present tense.
segments.p2-10-g1030: и is the coordinating conjunction 'and' connecting this participial phrase to what precedes. считавшаяся is a past passive/reflexive participle (from считаться) agreeing with the implied noun (the statue) and functioning as a qualifying clause meaning 'considered' or 'reckoned'. одним из семи чудес света is a noun phrase in the instrumental/genitive construction 'one of the seven wonders of the world' (из + genitive), completing the participial idea.
segments.p2-10-g1031: стояла is the past tense, imperfective verb 'stood/was located' describing the statue's position. в + prepositional гавани indicates location 'in the harbour.' древнегреческого города is a genitive attribute 'of the ancient Greek city,' and Родоса is the genitive of the place name 'Rhodes,' all together specifying where it stood.
segments.p2-10-g1032: A single capital letter followed by a period functions here as a label or bibliographic/item marker (an isolated initial/section label). It is not a full word and carries no additional grammatical morphology in the sentence fragment.
segments.p2-10-g1033: Е. is another isolated capital letter with a period used as an abbreviation or item label (similar to 'A.' above). It stands alone as a bibliographic/annotation marker rather than a syntactic element of a clause.
segments.p2-10-g1034: This is the main noun phrase: the surname Anaevsky with birth–death years in parentheses. The dash sets off an appositive: автор литературных поделок 'the author of literary поделки.' Поделок is in genitive plural and here is used derogatorily ('shoddy literary products'/'literary contrivances').
segments.p2-10-g1035: бывших is a past participle in genitive plural agreeing with поделок and here means 'which had been.' предметом постоянных насмешек is an instrumental construction 'the object of constant mockery,' with постоянных (constant) modifying насмешек (mockery). в журналистике 1840-1860-х годов is a prepositional phrase giving the sphere and time ('in the press in the 1840s–1860s').
segments.p2-10-g1036: The dashes mark a parenthetical apposition; в брошюре is a prepositional phrase ('in a pamphlet') introducing where or in what form the author presented the work. Брошюре is prepositional singular (feminine) because of в.
segments.p2-10-g1037: названной is a past passive participle in the same case/gender as брошюре (prepositional, feminine singular) and means 'named' or 'called.' им is the agent (by him) that performs the naming. The quoted title Энхиридион любознательный follows as the name he gave the pamphlet; СПб is the standard abbreviation for Saint Petersburg, here marking place/publication.
segments.p2-10-g1065: Roman numeral used as a heading: 'III' marks the third unit (here, the number of a chapter or section). It stands alone as a label and is followed by a comma in the original.
segments.p2-10-g1066: Abbreviation: 'гл.' = 'глава' (chapter). It's an abbreviated nominative label that pairs with the Roman numeral to mean 'chapter'.
segments.p2-10-g1067: Roman numeral (six) followed by a closing parenthesis, continuing the citation/heading begun in the previous segment — a label indicating chapter/section VI.
segments.p2-10-g1071: Subject + verb in the present indicative: 'Гейне' (Heine) is the nominative subject and 'утверждает' is 3rd-person singular present, meaning 'asserts' or 'maintains'. The comma introduces the subordinate clause with 'что'.
segments.p2-10-g1072: 'что' introduces the content clause ('that ...'); 'верные автобиографии' is the plural noun phrase (adjective + noun) in the nominative as the subject of the predicate 'невозможны'; 'почти' is an adverb modifying 'невозможны' ('almost'); the tilde here functions like an em dash setting up the example 'Руссо'.
segments.p2-10-g1073: Adverb 'например' = 'for example', used to mark the following proper name as an illustrative instance.
segments.p2-10-g1074: Past tense clause about Rousseau: 'непременно' (adverb) = 'certainly/necessarily'; 'налгал' is past masculine singular of 'налгать' (to lie) and here takes the construction 'налгал на себя' meaning 'lied about/to himself'; 'в своей исповеди' is a prepositional phrase 'in his Confessions', where 'своей' is the reflexive possessive agreeing with the subject (Rousseau).
segments.p2-10-g1077: 'Во' is the preposition 'в' in the form used before certain consonant clusters; 'во втором томе' = 'in the second volume' (locative/ prepositional). 'изданной во Франции' is a past passive participle phrase ('published in France') modifying 'книги' (genitive), and 'книги "О Германии"' = 'the book "On Germany"' (genitive because it is the volume of that book). Word order places the participial phrase between the noun's locative reference and the noun it modifies.
segments.p2-10-g1078: Prepositional phrase identifying the place within the work: 'в "Признаниях"' = 'in "(the) Confessions"'; the parenthetical years give the publication dates (1853–1854).
segments.p2-10-g1079: Simple reporting clause: 'Гейне' (Heine) is the nominative subject and 'писал' is the past tense (masculine) of 'писать' meaning 'wrote'; the colon introduces the quoted passage that follows.
segments.p2-10-g1080: Quoted clause begins: 'Составление' is a deverbal noun (the act of composing) in the nominative; 'собственной характеристики' is a genitive phrase 'of one's own character/characteristics' specifying what is being composed; 'было бы' combines the past tense 'было' with the conditional particle 'бы' to form a conditional ('would be'); 'работой' is the predicate in the instrumental case after 'быть' meaning 'a piece of work'; 'не только неудобной' continues the predicate with a short-form adjective in the instrumental, 'not only awkward'.
segments.p2-10-g1081: Continuation of the quoted predicate: 'но' = 'but'; 'попросту' = 'simply/plainly'; 'невозможной' (instrumental short-form adjective) continues the predicate complement 'impossible'; 'при всем желании' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with all desire' or 'even with every wish' (i.e., even if one were entirely willing); 'быть искренним' is an infinitive clause 'to be sincere' modifying 'желании'.
segments.p2-10-g1082: Final sentence of the quote: 'ни один человек' = 'no one / not a single person' (emphatic negative); 'не может' = 'cannot' (present tense, 3rd person); 'сказать правду' = 'to tell/say the truth'; 'о самом себе' = 'about himself', where 'самом' intensifies/reflexively points to the very self. The whole clause states an absolute impossibility.
segments.p2-10-g1038: Punctuation: a comma separating the closing parenthesis from the reporting verb; no lexical items to analyze here.
segments.p2-10-g1039: A bare year with a closing parenthesis (part of a previous parenthetical indicating publication details); functions as a date label before the verb 'писал'.
segments.p2-10-g1040: 'писал' is the past tense, masculine singular form of писать (imperfective) used here as a narrative reporting verb: 'he/it wrote' (introducing the quotation). The colon that follows marks the start of the quote.
segments.p2-10-g1041: A short, somewhat archaic clause: noun + adjective + archaic passive participle. 'Колосс Родосский' = 'the Colossus of Rhodes' (noun with adjective), and 'созижден' is an elevated/archaic passive meaning 'has been built/erected'. Word order places the predicate after the noun in a formal style.
segments.p2-10-g1042: 'некоторые писатели' is the nominative plural subject 'some writers', followed by the present-tense verb 'уверяют' (3rd person plural) meaning 'assert/claim'. The clause reports one group’s opinion.
segments.p2-10-g1043: An instrumental singular form 'Семирамидою' (archaic ending -ою) meaning 'by Semiramis' — the agent in the passive construction; here it specifies who some writers attribute the construction to.
segments.p2-10-g1044: Contrastive conjunction 'а' ('but/while') introducing the opposing group 'другие' ('others') and the present verb 'утверждают' (3rd plural) 'assert/maintain'. The colon prepares the reader for their version of the statement.
segments.p2-10-g1045: A predicative passive construction: 'он' (here 'it', referring back to the Colossus) + past passive participle 'воздвигнут' = 'it was erected/has been erected'. The negation 'не' modifies the following agent phrase 'рукою человеческою' (instrumental: 'by human hand'), where both 'рукою' and 'человеческою' show an archaic -ою instrumental ending.
segments.p2-10-g1046: Conjunction 'а' ('but') introduces the contrasting agent 'природою' (instrumental, archaic -ою) meaning 'by nature'; the closing quote and then '(с.' begins the bibliographic citation (abbreviated 'page').
segments.p2-10-g1047: Continuation and completion of the parenthetical citation started in the previous segment: the page number '96' followed by the closing parenthesis and period.
segments.p2-10-g1051: Exclamatory clause with object fronting: 'хоть' here has the sense 'at least/even', 'один кирпичик' is a diminutive 'one little brick' (accusative implied), 'на' + accusative/noun phrase 'такой капитальный дом' means 'for/on such a substantial/structural house' (purpose/for), and 'принесу' is 1st person singular future perfective 'I will bring'. The overall tone is emphatic willingness to contribute even a single small brick.
segments.p2-10-g1052: Adjective 'Полемический' ('polemical') modifies the noun 'намек' ('hint/allusion'); the phrase 'намек на выражение' uses the preposition 'на' with the accusative 'выражение' to mean 'an allusion to/an insinuation at a particular phrase'. The initial dash marks this as a commentary or aside.
segments.p2-10-g1053: Present passive/active participle 'встречающееся' (nominative, agreeing with 'выражение') meaning 'occurring/encountered', followed by the prepositional phrase 'в книгах' ('in the books') and the genitive phrase 'французского социалиста-утописта' = 'of the French utopian socialist' (author of those books). Word forms show agreement and the genitive attribute structure.
segments.p2-10-g1054: 'ученика' is genitive singular 'a disciple/pupil (of)', and 'Ш.' is an abbreviated initial (proper name implied). Together they mean 'a disciple of S.' and complete the attribution of the quoted expression.
segments.p2-10-g1114: A compact, slightly archaic measurement phrase: офицер is the head noun in the nominative (an officer). вершков is the genitive plural (the unit вершка) used as the measure, and росту is the dative/special-form noun used in constructions like “росту в …” to mean “of a height of …”; the whole phrase means “an officer of a height of ten vershoks.” Word order (measure before росту) is stylistically marked but grammatical.
segments.p2-10-g1117: По + dative is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning “according to”; установившейся is the past passive/participial adjective (feminine dative singular) from установить/установиться and modifies традиции (dative) — together: “according to the established tradition.” The phrase в XIX в. uses в + numeral + an abbreviated noun (в. = век) to mark time: “in the 19th century.”
segments.p2-10-g1118: рост is the subject noun “height.” обозначался is a reflexive/passive past-tense form (3rd person singular) of обозначать, so here “was indicated/was expressed.” вершками is the instrumental plural of вершок used to indicate the unit/measure (“in vershoks”).
segments.p2-10-g1119: которые is a plural relative pronoun agreeing with вершками (“which”). отмерялись is past passive/reflexive plural of отмерять meaning “were measured/determined.” сверх is a preposition meaning “beyond/in addition to” and governs the genitive, here двух аршин (“two arshins”), so the clause means “which were measured beyond two arshins.”
segments.p2-10-g1120: Следовательно is a conjunctive adverb meaning “therefore” or “consequently,” used to introduce a conclusion and set off by a comma.
segments.p2-10-g1121: рост этого офицера is a nominal phrase where этого (genitive of этот) modifies офицера, and together with рост forms “the height of this officer.” The dash replaces an explicit copula and links the subject to the measure phrase около 186 см.; около takes the genitive and here means “about/approximately.” The final см. is an abbreviation for сантиметров (centimetres).
segments.p2-10-g1152: This is an abbreviation: "примеч." stands for примечание (note/annotation). The final period marks the abbreviation; grammatically it functions as a nominative-heading label (short form used as a one-word sentence).
segments.p2-10-g1153: "к" is the preposition meaning 'to/toward' and governs the dative; here the following abbreviation "с." stands for (странице) — 'page' in the dative, so together they mean 'to page...'. Both letters carry periods as standard abbreviation marks.
segments.p2-10-g1154: An Arabic numeral indicating the page number 401, shown here with a closing parenthesis and then the sentence-final punctuation; there are no lexical words in this segment (it functions purely as a reference number).
segments.p2-10-g1158: Comparative form of the adjective бонтонный formed with the suffix -ее (бонтоннее) meaning 'more well-bred/fashionable'. Used here predicatively or as a brief evaluative comment; the final period ends the sentence.
segments.p2-10-g1055: Фурье is a surname presented as a bibliographic heading; here it stands alone followed by a comma as in an index or list. It is the proper name of the French utopian socialist (used as a heading, not inflected here).
segments.p2-10-g1056: В. is a capital letter plus period: an initial (abbreviation) of a given name. In Russian bibliographic style the initial follows the surname with a period.
segments.p2-10-g1057: Консидерана here appears as an author heading with life dates in parentheses and a colon introducing a quotation; it functions as a name in a bibliographic fragment. The form is presented as a heading (proper name) followed by dates.
segments.p2-10-g1058: This is a direct quotation in the first person. Несу is present-tense, first-person singular of нести ('I carry/bear'); свой is a reflexive/own possessive agreeing with the speaker; камень is the direct object in nominative within the quote; для governs the genitive здания ('for the building'), and будущего общества is a genitive noun phrase modifying зданиe, literally 'of the future society.'
segments.p2-10-g1059: Слова эти is a nominative plural noun phrase 'these words,' functioning as the grammatical subject of the following clause; the demonstrative эти identifies which words are meant.
segments.p2-10-g1060: Полемизируя is a present adverbial participle (деепричастие) meaning 'while polemicizing/arguing,' and it describes a concurrent action. It takes the instrumental construction with с + социалистами-утопистами ('with the utopian socialists'); социалистами and утопистами are in the instrumental plural as the objects of с.
segments.p2-10-g1061: Повторяют is an active present-tense plural verb ('they repeat'/'are repeated' in sense), agreeing with the grammatical subject Слова эти. В романе Достоевского 'Преступление и наказание' is an adverbial locative phrase ('in Dostoevsky's novel "Crime and Punishment"'). Разумихин follows the verb by inversion: the underlying sense is 'Razumikhin repeats these words' although the author places the words first and the verb agrees with them, so Разумихин is the agent named after the verb.
segments.p2-10-g1062: III is a Roman numeral used bibliographically to indicate part/section number (here 'Part/III'). It is not being used as an ordinary cardinal number but as a division label.
segments.p2-10-g1063: гл. is an abbreviation (глава) meaning 'chapter'; paired with the Roman numeral it forms a standard bibliographic reference (Part/Section III, chapter ...).
segments.p2-10-g1064: V) is a Roman-numeral item (closing/labeling a parenthetical reference). и is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the two referenced characters; Раскольников is a proper name in the nominative, and ч. is an abbreviation (часть/ч. or ч.) indicating 'part' or 'chapter'—this fragment continues a bibliographic citation.
segments.p2-10-g1137: This is the prepositional construction в + виде meaning “in the form/appearance.” Абличительном is the adjective абличительный in the prepositional singular (agreeing with вид) and is used here in quotes/ironic sense; the adjective is in the prepositional case because it follows в виде. The whole phrase functions as an adverbial phrase: “in an (so-called) ablichitelny form.”
segments.p2-10-g1140: The quoted word Абличительный is presented as the form being used; it stands before вместо, which normally governs the genitive (i.e. ‘вместо (чего?)’). Обличительный is cited as the replaced form. Здесь и ниже are adverbial modifiers (‘here and below/in the following passages’). Употреблено is the short/passive participle (neuter singular) of употребить used impersonally — it literally means “is/was used” and agrees with the implied noun (e.g. 'словo' or the quoted form). В ироническом смысле is a prepositional phrase meaning “in an ironic sense.”
segments.p2-10-g1141: Ср. is the common abbreviation of сравните (compare, cf.), an editorial cross-reference marker asking the reader to compare with another passage.
segments.p2-10-g1142: С. here is the abbreviation for страница (page). It stands alone as a bibliographic marker introducing the page number that follows.
segments.p2-10-g1143: A page number (360) that follows the abbreviation с.; there are no lexical items to analyze beyond its function as the referenced page.
segments.p2-10-g1144: A nominal statement using a dash as an equative/copular device: Мизер = нищета. The dash stands in place of the verb 'is' (это/есть) and links the foreign headword to its Russian gloss; the comma shows the list continues.
segments.p2-10-g1145: Убожество continues the glossing of Мизер in apposition; the opening parenthesis begins an etymological note. 'Франц.' is an abbreviated adjective meaning 'French' (i.e., indicating the source language).
segments.p2-10-g1146: The French original word written in Roman letters; here it appears inside the parenthetical begun in the previous segment and closed by the parenthesis and period. It is presented unchanged as the etymon.
segments.p2-10-g1147: Another foreign headword shown in Russian orthography; 'Cуперфлю' names the French term 'superflu' and the parenthetical 'франц.' again marks its language. The construction is parallel to the previous gloss.
segments.p2-10-g1148: The printed gloss gives the French form 'superflu' followed by a dash and the Russian equivalent 'лишний' — another equative apposition (superflu = лишний). The dash again functions as 'is' or 'equals'.
segments.p2-10-g1149: Бесполезный closes the parenthetical list of Russian equivalents (useless, futile); the closing parenthesis ends the gloss and the following dash introduces the commentator's own explanatory remark. 'Здесь' refers to the present contextual meaning ('in this passage').
segments.p2-10-g1150: Изысканная is a feminine singular adjective used predicatively/substantively in the commentary ('here: [meaning] — изысканная'), rendered as 'refined' or 'exquisite'. The semicolon separates this gloss from the following explanatory clause.
segments.p2-10-g1151: A full explanatory clause: 'в этом смысле' = 'in this sense' (в + demonstrative/locative 'этом' + noun 'смысле'); 'это слово было употреблено' is a passive perfect construction meaning 'this word was used' (было + past passive participle употреблено); 'Ноздревым' is the instrumental case meant to indicate the agent 'by Nozdrev' (the character), followed by a locative phrase 'в "Мёртвых душах" Гоголя' ('in Gogol's "Dead Souls"'). 'см.' is an abbreviation meaning 'see' or 'cf.'
segments.p2-10-g1173: A colon used here to introduce a citation or title; in Russian typography a colon can immediately precede a quoted title when referring the reader to a work. It connects the preceding parenthetical 'see' to the quoted item that follows.
segments.p2-10-g1174: A quoted book title (Подросток) followed by a full stop; the quotes mark it as the name of a work rather than a common noun. The period ends the sentence while the internal word is a noun used as a title.
segments.p2-10-g1175: An abbreviation (Ч.) for часть meaning 'part' in a bibliographic reference; the capital letter and period signal the shortened form. It denotes the major division of the work being cited.
