segments.p2-10-g1176: The numeral '1' with a following comma, indicating the number of the part given by the preceding abbreviation; it is a simple ordinal identifier in a citation.
segments.p2-10-g1177: An abbreviation (гл.) for глава meaning 'chapter'; used after the part number to introduce the chapter number that follows. The period marks the abbreviated form.
segments.p2-10-g1178: The numeral '5' with a comma, indicating the chapter number referred to by the preceding 'гл.'; it functions as the specific chapter identifier in the citation.
segments.p2-10-g1179: A Roman numeral 'III' (the third) followed by a closing parenthesis and a period; here it designates a subsection or numbered paragraph within the chapter and also closes the earlier parenthetical citation while ending the sentence.
segments.p2-10-g1089: A proper name in the nominative (Анненков) standing before a comma; it is the sentence's subject (explicitly named) and anchors the following appositive description and verb.
segments.p2-10-g1090: Two nouns in apposition to the name, both nominative singular and joined by и ('and'): 'критик' and 'мемуарист' describe Annenkov's roles; Russian omits articles, so this is a bare appositive noun phrase.
segments.p2-10-g1091: 'в статье' is a prepositional phrase (в + prepositional case) giving the location/context of the remark; the title in quotes names the article. The parenthetical (1849) gives its date. 'отметил' is past tense, masculine singular (perfective in form here functioning as the main verb), agreeing with the subject Анненков and meaning 'noted/remarked'.
segments.p2-10-g1092: A subordinate clause introduced by the conjunction 'что' ('that'). The quoted phrase 'Сырой дождик и мокрый снег' is the grammatical subject (two coordinated nouns). 'Являются' is the 3rd-person plural present of 'являться' (formal 'are'); it takes a predicate in the instrumental, here 'непременными элементами' ('indispensable elements' in instrumental plural). The rest is a locative modifier: 'петербургского пейзажа' is genitive ('of the Petersburg landscape'), and 'в повестях писателей "натуральной школы" и их подражателей' uses 'в' + prepositional plural with genitive 'писателей' and 'натуральной школы' naming the school and 'их подражателей' ('their imitators') as parallel genitives.
segments.p2-10-g1096: 'Охотясь' is a деепричастие (adverbial participle/gerund) expressing a simultaneous or attendant action ('hunting/pursuing'). 'тогда' is a temporal adverb ('then'). 'за' with instrumental (Костанжоглами, дядюшками Петрами Ивановичами) means 'after'/'in pursuit of'; the instrumental plural forms show the objects being pursued. The particle 'да' here is archaic/colloquial coordinating 'and' (linking the two objects).
segments.p2-10-g1099: Two noun phrases set in apposition around a dash: 'Образцовый хозяин' (the descriptive label) and 'помещик Костанжогло' (the specific landowner). 'Изображен' is a short passive participle/predicative meaning 'is depicted' (passive), and the agent is expressed in the instrumental 'Гоголем' ('by Gogol'). 'Во втором томе "Мертвых душ"' is a locative expression (во + prepositional) specifying where the depiction appears.
segments.p2-10-g1100: A character's full name in the nominative ('Петр Иванович Адуев') followed by a dash and the source phrase 'из романа' where 'из' requires the genitive 'романа' ('from the novel'); 'И.' is an abbreviated author initial. The construction simply indicates the character comes from that novel.
segments.p2-10-g1206: A standalone Arabic numeral used here as a year/date; grammatically it is an indeclinable cardinal numeral appearing as a complete sentence. The final period is ordinary sentence punctuation, marking the end of the item.
segments.p2-10-g1208: An Arabic numeral followed by a period used as a numbering marker (an enumerative or item number); syntactically it functions as a standalone numeral in an ordered list or citation. The trailing period is the punctuation convention that turns the numeral into an item label.
segments.p2-10-g1209: The single-letter abbreviation 'С.' (from 'страница/страницы') used in bibliographic contexts to indicate 'page' or 'pages'; it is an indeclinable abbreviation and is followed by a period as part of the abbreviation. Here it functions as the label that precedes a page number.
segments.p2-10-g1210: The Arabic numeral '37' followed by a closing parenthesis and a period; the number itself is a cardinal numeral (here serving as a page number or reference), the ')' closes a parenthetical expression, and the final '.' ends the sentence or citation.
segments.p2-10-g1201: Филантроп-преобразователь is a hyphenated compound noun: a subject label meaning 'philanthropist-reformer.' The phrase в утопии Кабе is a prepositional phrase giving location/possession ('in Cabet’s utopia'), with Кабе functioning as the name associated with the utopia. The dash marks a pause before the predicate; также (an adverb) precedes the verb разбивает (3rd person singular present), which is a literary/historic present here meaning 'routs/defeats.' The sequence в битве при Аустерлице uses в + noun (битве) and the preposition при + place name to mean 'in/at the Battle of Austerlitz.' См. is the standard abbreviation for 'see' (reference).
segments.p2-10-g1202: The single colon character functions as punctuation introducing the bibliographic citation that follows (common in footnotes and references).
segments.p2-10-g1203: Комарович В. is a standard Russian bibliographic author form: surname first, then the initial with a period. The initial stands for the author's given name.
segments.p2-10-g1204: Л. is an abbreviation used in citations, here standing for 'лист'/'leaf' or a page/leaf marker in an edition; it's presented as a single-letter abbreviated bibliographic item.
segments.p2-10-g1205: This is a bibliographic-title line: Юность Достоевского is the title of an article ('Youth of Dostoevsky') with Достоевского in the genitive indicating whose youth; the double slash // separates the article title from the periodical or source title Былое (the journal name). Titles are given in nominative/standalone form as is usual in citations.
segments.p2-10-g1125: как introduces a comparison meaning 'like' or 'as'; поручик Пирогов is a nominative noun phrase (rank + surname) used as the example being compared; у + genitive (у Гоголя) indicates 'in/with' the author (i.e. 'in Gogol's [work]').
segments.p2-10-g1126: The dash marks a brief, elliptic continuation; по takes the dative and here has the sense of recourse/direction toward a body or office, so по + dative начальству means 'to/through the superiors' or 'appealing to the authorities.'
segments.p2-10-g1127: This is a nominative appositive identifying the character: Поручик Пирогов (Lieutenant Pirogov). The prepositional phrase в повести Гоголя specifies location 'in Gogol's novella' (title follows in quotes); the date in parentheses is incidental.
segments.p2-10-g1128: после того как is a compound temporal conjunction meaning 'after (the fact) that' and introduces the subordinate clause. Он был высечен is a passive past construction (быть in past + short passive participle высечен = 'he was flogged'); the agent is expressed in the instrumental (оскорбленным мужем), and the dash plus apposition (немцем ремесленником) gives further identification of that agent.
segments.p2-10-g1129: хотел is past-tense of хотеть and expresses an intention or desire ('wanted to'); it governs two coordinated infinitives: жаловаться (to complain) with the dative генералу ('to the general'), and подать (to submit) which takes a direct object письменную просьбу ('a written petition') and a directional phrase в Главный штаб (в + accusative = toward the Headquarters). Quotation marks indicate a literal phrase being reported.
segments.p2-10-g1130: This is a dictionary-style definition: the headword Штафирка is followed by a dash and then the noun подкладка 'lining' or the phrase борт подкладки 'edge of the lining' (подкладки is genitive, modifying борт); в ботинках uses в + prepositional plural to mean 'in boots.'
segments.p2-10-g1131: юбках is another noun in the same case as ботинках (prepositional plural) and continues the list 'in boots, (or) skirts'; the semicolon ends the definitional part.
segments.p2-10-g1132: здесь + dash signals a shift to contextual meaning: 'here' (in this text) the word is used as a презрительное наименование 'a contemptuous designation' of штатских (the civilians). The abbreviated (нем. begins a parenthetical indicating the German original follows.
segments.p2-10-g1133: stafieren is the German infinitive given as the original; the dash defines it with the Russian infinitive отделывать 'to finish / to trim' (infinitive form). The closing parenthesis ends the parenthetical note.
segments.p2-10-g1161: The phrase begins with the comparative adverb более ('more') modifying the participial/adjectival form соответствующее ('suitable/corresponding'). Correspondующее is in a neutral form (agreeing with an omitted noun) and is followed by the dative/indirect object вкусу ('to the taste'), so the whole phrase means 'more appropriate to the taste of...'. Хорошего общества is a genitive attribute 'of good society' (i.e. polite society). The parenthetical abbreviation франц. starts here (short for французский, 'French'). The initial hyphen indicates this is an inserted remark continuing from the previous sentence.
segments.p2-10-g1162: The French loan bon ton is given followed by a dash and the Russian gloss хороший тон ('good tone'). Тон here means 'manner/style' (social tone), not sound. The closing parenthesis ends the explanatory aside.
segments.p2-10-g1166: The verbs are first-person singular present: получаю ('I receive/get') and жертвую ('I give/donate' or 'I sacrifice'), forming a coordinate clause joined by и. Несметные миллионы is a plural noun phrase ('countless millions') with несметные as an adjective. Тотчас же is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at once' or 'immediately', where же emphatically reinforces immediacy. Их is the pronoun 'them' (direct object, accusative) referring back to millions. На + accusative here (на род человеческий) expresses purpose/benefit: 'for the human race' (i.e. to humankind).
segments.p2-10-g1169: Эта мечта is the nominative subject 'this dream.' Подпольного героя is a genitive attribute ('of the "Underground" hero') attached to мечта (i.e. 'the hero's dream'). Возродилась is a past-tense, perfective, reflexive verb meaning 'was reborn' or 'reappeared.' Позднее is an adverb 'later.' В + dative/locative phrase в ... идее indicates 'in the ... idea'; ротшильдовской is an adjectival form ('Rothschildian' or indicating huge wealth) modifying идея; Подростка is in the genitive and names the work/character ('of The Adolescent'). Quotation marks signal ironic or literary labels.
segments.p2-10-g1170: Который is a relative pronoun ('who') referring back to Подростка; тоже means 'also' or 'likewise'; хотел is the past-tense verb 'wanted.' Together they form the relative clause 'who also wanted,' linking to the infinitive that follows.
segments.p2-10-g1171: Скопив and насладившись are perfective gerunds (деепричастия) meaning 'having amassed' and 'having enjoyed,' respectively; they describe actions completed prior to the main action (wanted to give). Огромное богатство is the direct object of скопив ('enormous wealth'), and могуществом is in the instrumental (the case used with насладиться) meaning 'power/might.' The gerunds are linked by и.
segments.p2-10-g1172: Отдать is the infinitive 'to give/hand over' that complements хотел ('wanted to give'). Свои миллионы is a possessive noun phrase ('his/one's own millions'); людям is the dative plural indicating the recipients ('to the people'). См. is an abbreviation for 'see' (compare).
segments.p2-10-g1183: чего-то is чего (interrogative/relative 'what') with the particle -то to form an indefinite pronoun meaning 'something' (genitive/partitive flavor in this elliptical phrase). манфредовского is an adjectival form derived from Manfred (manfredovsky) used substantively/adjectivally to mean 'Manfred-like' or 'of Manfred'; here it echoes the indefinite 'чего-то' to mean 'something Manfred-like.'
segments.p2-10-g1186: здесь is an adverb meaning 'here' (in this passage / in this respect) pointing to the immediately following characterization; the dash marks a sharp appositive or clarification.
segments.p2-10-g1187: чего-то again is the indefinite 'something' formed from чего + -то (genitive/partitive sense implied). гордого is the adjective гордый in the same case (genitive/accusative form used substantively after чего-то) meaning 'proud' (in the noble/sublime sense) and modifies the implied thing 'something proud.'
segments.p2-10-g1188: возвышенного is the adjective возвышенный in the same nominalized/partitive form as гордого, meaning 'sublime' or 'elevated'; it adds a second quality to the indefinite 'something.'
segments.p2-10-g1189: Манфред is the subject (proper name). The dash functions as an equative/copular pause: 'is.' герой одноименной драматической поэмы Байрона reads as a noun phrase in the genitive after 'герой' (герой + genitive of the thing he is hero of): 'hero of the eponymous dramatic poem by Byron.' Байрона is in the genitive to indicate authorship/possession; (1817) is a parenthetical date.
segments.p2-10-g1190: в которой = 'in which' (в + relative pronoun которой, feminine singular to agree with поэмы). нашла is the past tense, feminine singular of найти and agrees with the feminine subject философия: here it means 'found' (i.e., 'was given' or 'found expression'). отражение is the noun 'reflection/representation' as the object of нашла (нашла отражение = 'found expression/is reflected'). философия is the nominative subject; мировой скорби is a genitive phrase ('of world-sorrow'/'world-weariness') modifying философия.
segments.p2-10-g1194: разбиваю is 1st person singular present of разбивать meaning here 'I rout/defeat' (a martial, energetic verb). ретроградов is the animate accusative plural (form identical to the genitive) of ретроград, meaning 'reactionaries' or 'retrogrades'—the direct object. под + Аустерлицем uses под with the instrumental (Аустерлицем) to indicate location/time of a battle: 'at/near Austerlitz.'
segments.p2-10-g1197: The clause begins with the adverbial phrase 'Здесь и далее' meaning 'here and further/onward' (literally 'here and later') that sets a textual scope. 'герой представляет себя' uses the verb 'представлять(ся)' in the 3rd person singular present indicative with the reflexive 'себя' to mean 'imagines/portrays himself'; 'в роли' + accusative expresses 'in the role of' and is followed by the proper name in the accusative 'Наполеона I'. The initial dash marks this as a parenthetical or editorial remark.
segments.p2-10-g1198: 'В данном случае' is a fixed prepositional phrase ('in this case') where 'данном' agrees in gender/case with 'случае'. 'имеется в виду' is an impersonal verbal phrase in the present tense meaning 'is meant/is referred to' (literally 'is had in view'); the real subject of the clause follows: 'победа Наполеона I' (the victory of Napoleon I). 'под' with the instrumental 'Аустерлицем' expresses location in the sense 'at/in the battle of Austerlitz'. The sequence of numbers and months gives the date (old style/new style) followed by the abbreviated 'г.' for 'year'.
segments.p2-10-g1199: This fragment continues the previous noun 'победа' and uses the preposition 'над' + instrumental to indicate 'over' (as in victory over someone). 'объединенными' is a participle used adjectivally (instrumental plural) agreeing with 'войсками'; 'русско-австрийскими' is a compound adjective in instrumental plural meaning 'Russian–Austrian'; 'войсками' is the instrumental plural of 'войско' ('troops/army').
segments.p2-10-g1200: 'В мечтах героя' is a prepositional phrase ('in the hero's dreams') with 'мечтах' in the prepositional plural; here it refers to the hero's fantasies/daydreams. The subject 'исследователи' (researchers) takes the past-tense verb 'обнаружили' ('discovered/detected'), and 'некоторую перекличку с историей Икара' means 'a certain correspondence/echo with the story of Icarus' ('перекличку' metaphorically indicates a thematic echo). The final noun phrase 'в социально-утопическом романе Кабе "Путешествие в Икарию" (1840)' specifies where that story appears: 'in the social-utopian novel by Cabet, Voyage to Icaria (1840)'.
segments.p2-10-g1226: бал is a noun meaning 'ball' or 'dance' functioning as the head of this noun phrase; для + genitive (всей Италии) marks purpose/benefit 'for all Italy' (всей is the genitive singular of весь here meaning 'all of'); на вилле Боргезе is a locative prepositional phrase ('на' + prepositional вилле) meaning 'at the Villa Borghese', with the proper name Боргезе following the noun.
segments.p2-10-g1227: что is a relative pronoun 'which/that' referring back to 'вилла(Боргезе)'; на берегу uses на + a locative/locative-variant (берегу) meaning 'on the shore'; озера is the genitive of озеро so озера Комо = 'of Lake Como' (Комо specifies which lake).
segments.p2-10-g1230: Очевидно is an adverb ('obviously'/'apparently') used as a discourse marker; the dash indicates reported speech or an aside and the adverb qualifies the following clause.
segments.p2-10-g1231: имеется в виду is an impersonal fixed expression meaning 'is meant/is referred to' (имеется is the impersonal/present form of иметься and в виду is the idiomatic complement); празднование is the noun 'celebration' that is the thing being meant; в 1806 г. is a temporal prepositional phrase 'in 1806' (в with a year, г. = abbreviation for 'year').
segments.p2-10-g1232: основания is the genitive singular of основание here meaning 'the founding/of the foundation' and functions as the object of 'празднование' in the previous clause ('the celebration of the founding'); французской империи is a genitive noun phrase 'of the French Empire' with французской agreeing in case, number, and gender with империи.
segments.p2-10-g1233: которое is a neuter singular relative pronoun agreeing with основания (neuter); было приурочено is a past-tense passive construction ('was scheduled/assigned'), where приурочено is the short passive participle; к 15 августа uses к + date (августа in genitive) meaning 'for/on August 15' or 'scheduled for August 15.'
segments.p2-10-g1234: дню is the dative singular of день used appositively after the date (к 15 августа, дню...)—a common pattern 'scheduled for August 15, the day of ...'; рождения is a genitive noun 'of birth' forming 'day of the birth' (i.e., 'birthday'); Наполеона is the genitive of Наполеон, specifying whose birthday it is; I is the ordinal 'the First.'
segments.p2-10-g1235: Вилла Боргезе is a proper-name noun phrase 'Villa Borghese' (a named villa/gallery); в Риме is a locative prepositional phrase ('in Rome') indicating location, with Риме in the prepositional case.
segments.p2-10-g1236: основанная is a past passive participle (long form) used attributively and agreeing with a feminine noun (here the omitted 'villa'); it functions like an adjective meaning 'founded'. 'в первой половине' is a temporal prepositional phrase ('in the first half') with в + the noun половина in the prepositional; VII в. is an abbreviation for 'веке' (century) given by the Roman numeral.
segments.p2-10-g1237: A punctuation mark introducing the participial phrase; it shows that the following clause is a parenthetical modifier of the previous noun.
segments.p2-10-g1238: украшенная is a past passive participle (feminine) functioning as an adjective 'adorned'. The phrase изящными постройками uses the instrumental plural (with the adjective also in instrumental) to indicate the means/objects with which it was adorned ('with elegant buildings').
segments.p2-10-g1239: A continuation of the instrumental list: фонтанами и статуями are nouns in instrumental plural coordinated by и, naming additional items used to adorn the place ('with fountains and statues').
segments.p2-10-g1240: принадлежала is the past tense (feminine singular) of принадлежать 'to belong'; the owner is expressed after the verb in the dative (Камилло Боргезе). В это время is a temporal adverbial phrase 'at this time/at that time'.
segments.p2-10-g1241: за которым is a relative phrase using за + instrumental (которым refers back to the masculine person 'Camillo') and introduces the clause 'to whom ... was married'. была замужем is the fixed past-tense predicate meaning 'was married' (said of a woman). сестра Наполеона I Полина is the nominative subject ('the sister of Napoleon I, Pauline'), with Наполеона in the genitive showing possession.
segments.p2-10-g1242: Озеро Кома is a nominative noun phrase (subject). расположено is the short passive/neuter predicate 'is situated/located', agreeing with the neuter noun озеро. в Итальянских Альпах is a locative prepositional phrase (в + prepositional plural) giving the location.
segments.p2-10-g1246: у is a preposition meaning 'at/near/by' used with the genitive to indicate location. Пяти углов (here genitive plural) names a place ('Five Corners'); углов is the genitive plural of угол 'corner'.
segments.p2-10-g1270: "Не" is the negation particle placed before the prepositional phrase; "для" is the preposition that governs the genitive, and "детей" is the genitive plural of ребёнок (children). The whole phrase in quotes functions as a title: "Not for Children" (note the closing quotation mark and following comma).
segments.p2-10-g1271: «выходивший» is the past active participle (—вший) from выходить, used attributively (masculine singular form) meaning "which appeared/was published" and links to an implied noun in the context (e.g. листок). "в 1862 г." uses the preposition в with a year to mean "in 1862," and «г.» is the common abbreviation for год (year).
segments.p2-10-g1272: «и» is the coordinating conjunction "and." «цинично» is an adverb modifying the participle, meaning "cynically." «проповедовавший» is another past active participle (—вший) from проповедовать, functioning adjectivally here as "who preached/advocated" and matching the same implied noun; it takes the following noun as its direct object. «беспринципность» is the noun meaning "lack of principles," used as the object of проповедовавший (in accusative, identical in form to nominative).
segments.p2-10-g1273: «Гальбик» is the subject (a proper name). The dash functions as a copular link (like "is"). «азартная» (adjective "gambling") and «карточная» (adjective "card-related") premodify the noun «игра» (noun "game"), together forming the predicate noun phrase "a gambling card game." Word order is adjective–adjective–noun as usual in Russian.
segments.p2-10-g1277: «все это» = "all this/these things." «из» is the preposition "from," which requires the genitive; here it introduces the source(s). «Сильвио» is a name used as the source (invariable in form here). The second «из» repeats the preposition for the next source; «"Маскарада"» is the genitive form of the title "Masquerade," and «Лермонтова» is the genitive of the author Lermontov ("of Lermontov"). Together the phrase means "all this comes from Silvio and from Lermontov's 'Masquerade'."
segments.p2-10-g1214: папа is the nominative subject ('the Pope'). соглашается is the present tense, 3rd person singular of соглашаться with the reflexive ending -ся, meaning 'agrees/consents' (verb + reflexive). выехать is a perfective infinitive 'to depart/leave (by vehicle or travel away)'; из Рима (из + genitive) gives the point of departure 'from Rome', and в Бразилию (в + accusative) marks motion toward the destination 'to Brazil'. Word order is Subject — Verb — infinitive phrase — source — destination.
segments.p2-10-g1217: Конфликт is nominative 'conflict'. Наполеона I is a genitive attributive ('of Napoleon I'), with the Roman numeral I appended as an identifier. с + папой Пием VII uses the preposition с with the instrumental to mean 'with Pope Pius VII' (папой and Пием are in instrumental; VII is a numeral apposition). The phrase is a nominal phrase introducing the subject matter.
segments.p2-10-g1218: в результате is a fixed prepositional expression 'as a result' (в + prepositional of результат). которого is a relative pronoun in the genitive ('of which' / 'as a result of which'), linking to 'конфликт'. Наполеон I был отлучен is a passive construction using был (past of быть) + short passive participle отлучен 'was excommunicated'. в 1809 г. is a time expression 'in 1809' (в + year + abbreviation for год).
segments.p2-10-g1219: от + noun in genitive (церкви) is a simple prepositional phrase meaning 'from the Church' or 'from the Church institution'; here it completes 'отлучен от церкви' ('excommunicated from the Church'), with от indicating separation/origin.
segments.p2-10-g1220: а is a contrastive conjunction meaning 'but/and' (introducing the parallel statement). папа Пий VII is the nominative subject 'Pope Pius VII' (Пий is the name; VII a numeral). в течение пяти лет is a temporal phrase 'for five years' (в течение + genitive лет). был is past tense 'was'; фактическим узником uses the instrumental case for a predicative complement ('a de facto prisoner') — фактическим here means 'de facto/actual' and узником is 'prisoner'; французского императора is a genitive attribute 'of the French emperor.'
segments.p2-10-g1221: закончился is the past reflexive verb 'ended' (закончиться) and here takes an instrumental argument to indicate how it ended. возвращением is the instrumental noun 'with the return' (закончиться чем? — возвращением). в 1814 г. is a time adverbial 'in 1814' (в + year + г. for год).
segments.p2-10-g1222: Пия is the accusative form of the name Пий ('Pius'), used here elliptically (the verb 'returned' is omitted but implied); VII is the numeral apposition. в + accusative (Рим) marks motion toward a place, so в Рим means 'to Rome.' The short fragment thus expresses '[the return of] Pius VII to Rome.'
segments.p2-10-g1278: This is a nominal (copula-less) sentence: the dash stands in for the present-tense verb 'is' (быть) and links subject and predicative noun phrase. 'Сильвио' is the subject (nominative). 'главный персонаж' is the predicate noun phrase in apposition, and 'повести А.' is a genitive phrase ('of the novella by A.') specifying which novella; 'А.' is an author abbreviation.
segments.p2-10-g1279: A single-letter abbreviation with period: 'С.' stands for 'страница/страницы' (page/pages) in bibliographic notation. It appears here as an isolated bibliographic marker rather than a full word.
segments.p2-10-g1280: 'Пушкина' is the genitive of 'Пушкин' and marks possession: 'Pushkin's'. It modifies the quoted title '"Выстрел"' (the work's title), with the year in parentheses as a publication date; the comma signals the following relative clause.
segments.p2-10-g1281: 'вся жизнь' ('the whole life') is the subject of the relative clause; 'которого' is the genitive relative pronoun ('of whom/of which') referring back to the (masculine) character just mentioned. 'была посвящена' is the short-form passive predicate in the past tense ('was devoted'), and 'идее мести' uses dative ('to the idea') plus a genitive attribute ('of revenge').
segments.p2-10-g1282: 'В конце повести' is a temporal prepositional phrase ('at the end of the novella'). 'он' is the pronoun subject. 'восторжествовал' is a past-tense, perfective verb meaning 'triumphed' (completed action). 'над своим противником' uses the preposition 'над' with the instrumental to mean 'over his opponent'; 'своим' is the reflexive/possessive adjective pointing back to the subject ('his/one's own').
segments.p2-10-g1283: В драме — prepositional phrase formed with в + noun in the prepositional case (драме) meaning 'in the play'; the quoted title Маскарад stands after it in nominative as an apposition/title. Аналогичная роль — noun phrase in the nominative (аналогичная agrees with роль: feminine, singular) serving as the grammatical subject. Принадлежит — 3rd person singular present indicative of принадлежать, a verb that takes the recipient in the dative; it means 'belongs to' or 'pertains to'. Неизвестному — dative singular (ending -ому) of Неизвестный here used as the recipient of принадлежит; capitalized, it functions as a name or epithet 'the Unknown.'
segments.p2-10-g1284: И — simple coordinating conjunction 'and' (here adding or introducing the quoted imperative). В дом мой — preposition в with the accusative (дом) indicating motion into someone’s house; мой is the possessive adjective 'my' agreeing with дом. Смело и свободно — two adverbial modifiers linked by и, modifying the imperative that follows ('boldly and freely'). Хозяйкой полною — instrumental case: хозяйкой is the noun 'hostess/mistress' in instrumental, used as a role or manner ('as mistress'); полною is an archaic/poetic instrumental singular form of полный agreeing with хозяйкой. Войди — 2nd-person singular imperative of войти (perfective), a direct command or invitation to enter; its archaic/poetic flavor suits the quoted verse.
segments.p2-10-g1285: Заключительные строки — adjective + noun (both plural) meaning 'the final/concluding lines'; here this phrase introduces the quotation. Стихотворения Некрасова — two genitives: стихотворения (genitive singular of 'poem') governed by строки ('lines of the poem') and Некрасова (genitive singular of the author's surname) showing authorship. Когда из мрака заблужденья — the opening words of the poem used as the title/quotation; Когда is 'when', and the preposition из governs the genitive мрака ('from the darkness'); заблужденья is the (archaic-spelled) genitive singular of заблужденье 'delusion', forming the phrase 'from the darkness of delusion.' Note the slightly archaic spelling/ending -енья in заблужденья.
segments.p2-10-g1288: Parenthetical year: a date in parentheses indicating the year (here, likely the poem's publication or composition date). It's not grammatically inflected—just a numeral used as a chronological reference.
segments.p2-10-g1259: This is an appositive equating the French term with a Russian noun phrase: two adjectives precede and agree with the noun 'обычай' (nominative singular masculine). The dash stands in for a copula ('is' / 'means').
segments.p2-10-g1260: 'так называемое' is a fixed descriptive phrase meaning 'so-called'; 'называемое' is a participial/adjectival form here modifying the neuter noun 'право' (hence 'так называемое право'). 'первой ночи' is a genitive attribute: 'right of the first night' (ordinal 'первой' agrees with feminine 'ночи').
segments.p2-10-g1261: 'по которому' is a relative phrase ('under/according to which') where 'которому' is dative (referring back to 'право'). 'Крепостная крестьянка' is the nominative subject ('a serf peasant woman'). 'Обязана была' is a compound predicate: short-form adjective 'обязана' (feminine) meaning 'obliged' with the past auxiliary 'была' to place the obligation in the past. The infinitive 'проводить' means 'to spend (time)' and governs the direct object 'первую брачную ночь' (accusative). 'Со своим господином' uses the preposition 'со' + instrumental to mean 'with her lord.'
segments.p2-10-g1265: 'ненавижу' is 1st person singular present ('I hate'). The two objects are coordinated by 'и': 'клубничку' is accusative singular (direct object) and 'клубничников' is an animate plural form whose accusative looks like the genitive (hence the -ов ending); both are direct objects of 'ненавижу.'
segments.p2-10-g1266: 'Под' followed by the instrumental ('клубничкой') forms the idiom 'под X подразумевать Y' — 'to mean/under X is meant Y.' 'Гоголевский Ноздрев' is the subject ('Gogol's Nozdrev'); 'подразумевал' is past tense, masculine singular ('meant/was implying'); 'любовные похождения' is the accusative plural object meaning 'amorous escapades.'
segments.p2-10-g1267: 'Здесь' is an adverb meaning 'here' or 'in this place/passage' and is used sentence-initially as a discourse marker.
segments.p2-10-g1268: 'возможно' is a parenthetical adverb ('possibly') expressing uncertainty about the identification that follows.
segments.p2-10-g1269: The phrase 'имеется в виду' is an impersonal construction meaning 'is meant' or 'is intended.' Word order places 'имеется в виду' before the nominative noun 'бульварный листок' (the thing that is meant). 'Бульварный листок' is a noun phrase ('sensational/tabloid sheet') and 'Петербургская клубничка' is the title in apposition (feminine noun 'клубничка' governed by the adjective 'Петербургская').
segments.p2-10-g1249: Место (nominative singular) is the head noun meaning 'place' and is followed by the locative phrase в Петербурге. Петербурге is in the prepositional case (–е ending) after the preposition в, indicating location 'in Petersburg.' The initial dash marks spoken or parenthetical address.
segments.p2-10-g1250: Где introduces a relative clause 'where'; сходятся is 3rd-person plural present of сходиться meaning 'meet/converge' and agrees with the coordinated subjects that follow. The verb appears before the noun phrase (inversion), which is acceptable in Russian. Загородный проспект is a proper-name noun phrase: Загородный is an adjectival element of the street name and проспект is the noun 'avenue.'
segments.p2-10-g1251: Чернышев переулок is a compound proper name: the adjective-like element Чернышев (a surname) modifies переулок 'lane.' Parenthetical ныне ул. signals a current name ('now, ul.'), with ул. as the standard abbreviation for улица 'street.' The phrase is nominative as part of the list of streets that 'meet.'
segments.p2-10-g1252: Ломоносова here is the surname Ломоносов in the form used as a street-name element (historically written in the genitive after ул.), and it closes the previous parenthesis. The trailing comma belongs to the larger enumerative syntax.
segments.p2-10-g1253: Разъезжая и Троицкая улицы lists two street names: each adjective-like word (Разъезжая, Троицкая) is the fixed part of a street name and is followed by the head noun улицы in the plural to cover both items. Note that these adjective-forms remain in their lexical (feminine) forms because they are parts of proper names; последняя ('the latter') refers back to the second named street (Троицкая улица) and is feminine singular. Ныне ul. again indicates the current name in parentheses.
segments.p2-10-g1254: Рубинштейна is the surname Рубинштейн in the grammatical form used for street names (genitive-like usage after ул.), completing the parenthetical remark 'now Rubinshtein (Street)'. The closing parenthesis and period end the enumeration.
segments.p2-10-g1255: Акциз is the nominative head-word; the dash (—) introduces an appositive definition: 'is (a)'. Государственный налог is a nominative noun phrase 'state tax.' На + продукты takes the accusative (продукты) to mark what the tax is levied on, while широкого потребления is a genitive attribute 'of wide consumption' modifying продукты.
segments.p2-10-g1256: В данном случае is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'in this case'; данном is the demonstrative in prepositional singular agreeing with случае, which is in the prepositional case after в and functions adverbially.
segments.p2-10-g1257: Очевидно is an adverbial modal comment meaning 'obviously' or 'apparently'; it is parenthetical here and qualifies the speaker's assessment of the following phrase.
segments.p2-10-g1258: На вино is the preposition на + accusative noun вино, indicating the object of the tax: 'on wine.' The period ends the sentence.
segments.p2-10-g1292: "сложенный" is the past passive participle (причастие) of сложить used adjectivally meaning 'formed' or 'composed'; it agrees implicitly with a noun understood from context. "ижицей" is the instrumental singular of ижица and indicates the means or instrument ('by/with the izhitsa') — together: 'formed by the izhitsa.'
segments.p2-10-g1295: The word "треугольником" is the instrumental singular of треугольник used predicatively after a dash to mean 'in the shape of / as a triangle.' Inside the parenthesis, "ижица" is nominative and begins a gloss: the dash after it introduces a definition. "последняя буква" is a nominative noun phrase 'the last letter,' and "церковнославянской и старой русской азбуки" is a genitive phrase 'of the Church Slavonic and old Russian alphabet' modifying "буква." The conjunction "и" simply links the two adjectives/genres.
segments.p2-10-g1296: "обозначающая" is the present active participle (feminine singular) of обозначать and agrees with "буква" from the previous segment: 'the letter (which) denotes.' "звук" here is the noun 'sound' (the phonetic value) and combines with the quoted letter "и" to mean 'the sound "i".' The participle functions as an attributive clause: 'the letter ... denoting the sound "i".'
segments.p2-10-g1297: "Шамбр-гарни" is a transliteration of the French phrase chambres-garnies; the hyphenated form is treated as a fixed foreign term. The parenthetical abbreviation "(франц." is short for французский ('French') and signals that the original term comes from French.
segments.p2-10-g1298: "chmbres-garnies" is the French term (here closing the parenthesis), followed by a dash and the Russian gloss. "меблированные" is the past passive participle used adjectivally meaning 'furnished,' and "комнаты" is nominative plural 'rooms' — together 'furnished rooms.'
segments.p2-10-g761: Впоследствии is an adverb meaning 'afterwards/subsequently' placed at the sentence start to set the time frame. "Записки из подполья" is the book title in nominative (Записки) with the prepositional phrase из подполья ('from/of the underground') specifying the title; together they form the plural subject that agrees with the past plural verb привлекли. Привлекли is the past tense, perfective, 3rd person plural of привлечь ('to attract'), governing the direct object особое внимание ('special attention'). Н. is an abbreviated initial (here part of a person’s name in the genitive chain N. K. Mikhaylovsky) indicating whose attention was attracted; in Russian the person whose attention is attracted is commonly expressed by a genitive/name phrase following внимание.
segments.p2-10-g762: К. is a single-letter initial with a period, an anonymizing/middle initial that continues the abbreviated name begun by 'Н.' in the previous segment; it stands in the genitive-name chain (N. K. Mikhaylovsky) and is presented as a separate fragment for stylistic/printing reasons.
segments.p2-10-g763: Михайловского is the surname Mikhaylovsky in the genitive singular (ending -а), closing the abbreviated name phrase 'Н. К. Михайловского' that names the person whose attention was attracted. The following comma introduces a participial (relative) clause describing him.
segments.p2-10-g764: Посвятившего is a past active participle (посвятивший → посвятившего to agree in case) modifying Михайловского; because Михайловского is in the genitive, the participle appears in the corresponding form. Их разбору is a noun phrase meaning 'to their analysis' where их is a possessive/genitive pronoun ('their') modifying разбору (разбор in dative: the indirect object of посвятить — 'to devote to/for'), and специальный раздел is the direct object ('a special section') in accusative; в статье introduces the location 'in the article' and "Жестокий талант" is the article's title, followed by the publication year in parentheses.
segments.p2-10-g765: С is the preposition 'from/since' requiring the genitive that follows; конца is the genitive singular of конец ('end'), and XIX в. is the abbreviated time expression 'the 19th century' (XIX = nineteenth, в. = век/century), so the whole phrase means 'since/from the end of the 19th century.'
segments.p2-10-g1340: This is a bibliographic name: surname followed by an initial. The initial 'Ф.' (capital letter + period) is an abbreviated given name (here Фёдор). The opening parenthesis is literal and indicates this element is part of a citation or heading.
segments.p2-10-g1341: A single capital letter with a period is an initial or abbreviation; in Russian bibliographic entries this follows the first initial and usually stands for the patronymic (e.g. 'М.' for 'Михайлович'). Grammatically it functions as an abbreviated name element in the nominative citation.
segments.p2-10-g1342: 'Полн.' is an abbreviated adjective (short for 'полное' or 'полный'), used in bibliographic phrases to mean 'complete' or 'full'; it stands in the nominative to modify the following noun. The period marks truncation of the full word.
segments.p2-10-g1343: 'собр.' is an abbreviation of 'собрание' (a noun meaning 'collection' or 'collected works'); the period shows it's truncated. In bibliographic use it combines with the previous adjective ('Полн.') to form 'полное собрание' = 'complete collection/collected works.'
segments.p2-10-g1319: Хомякова is a family name in its citation form (here written with the final -а). In Russian bibliographical style the surname stands first, followed by a comma; the -а ending indicates either a feminine surname or the specific orthography of this family name rather than a grammatical case change.
segments.p2-10-g1320: Ю. is an initial (abbreviated given name) written with a capital letter and a period; it follows the surname after the comma and indicates the author's first name (e.g., Юрий or Юлия) in abbreviated form.
segments.p2-10-g1321: С. is an abbreviation (period included) most likely for страница/страницы ('page'/'pages') in bibliographic notation; here it appears alone as the abbreviated label that would normally precede a page number.
segments.p2-10-g1322: Самарина is a family name in citation form (the final -а is the orthographic ending of this surname); as with other entries it is followed by a comma to separate it from the initial of the given name.
segments.p2-10-g1323: И. is an abbreviated initial for a given name (e.g., Иван/Ирина), written as a single capital letter with a period and placed after the surname in standard bibliographic style.
segments.p2-10-g1324: В. is a single-letter initial (capital plus period) functioning as an abbreviated given name or initial; in a list it serves the same role as Ю. or И. and stands alone here as the abbreviated name element.
segments.p2-10-g1299: This whole segment is a bibliographic label: the quoted title in quotes is followed by a dash and a source. Жалкие слова is a noun phrase (adjective + plural noun) used as a title; Цитата is a nominative noun meaning “quotation,” and из + Obломова is the preposition + genitive that marks the source (“from Oblomov”). The parenthetical year is a date; the final single-letter with a period is an abbreviation/initial appended as a bibliographic mark.
segments.p2-10-g1300: А. here is a single-letter abbreviation/initial used in the text (likely an author’s initial or a shortened signature). It stands alone as a printed initial or marker rather than a full word.
segments.p2-10-g1301: This is a name used as an attribution or label (like an editor's or commentator's signature) and stands in the nominative with a colon to introduce the following remark. The form "Гончарова" is a surname in the feminine nominative (not a declined part of the sentence) and functions like "Goncharova:" or "Goncharova writes:".
segments.p2-10-g1302: "так" is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' and modifies the verb "называл"; the clause has inverted word order with the verb "называл" (past imperfective, 3rd person singular masculine) before the subject "Захар". "называл" is a transitive past-tense action 'called/named', and "назидательные поучения" is the direct object in the accusative (plural) meaning 'edifying/didactic admonitions'.
segments.p2-10-g1303: This is a relative clause modifying "назидательные поучения": "с которыми" is the preposition "с" + relative pronoun in the instrumental plural agreeing with "поучения" ('with which'). "обращался" is past imperfective of "обращаться" used with "к" to mean 'addressed' or 'appealed to' ("обращался к нему" = 'addressed himself to him'). "его барин" means 'his barin/master', where the possessive "его" refers back to Захар. "см." is the abbreviation "see" (used to point to a citation or note).
segments.p2-10-g1304: An abbreviation (here printed as a single letter with a period) serving as an editorial or bibliographic shorthand; in context it likely abbreviates a word such as "часть" (part) or "час"/"chapter" depending on the edition. It stands alone as a label.
segments.p2-10-g1315: This is an adjectival phrase in nominative case used as a quoted title or label. Живая is the feminine nominative singular adjective agreeing with жизнь (no copula — Russian often omits 'to be' in titles), and the quotation marks indicate this is a quoted phrase rather than a full clause.
segments.p2-10-g1316: Понятие is the nominative singular subject 'the notion'; the quoted phrase "живая жизнь" is in apposition to that subject and therefore also nominative. Было распространено is a past‑tense neuter short passive construction (было + short passive participle) meaning 'was widespread/was disseminated'; в + prepositional (литературе, публицистике) indicates the fields where it was common, and XIX в. gives the temporal specification ('in the 19th century').
segments.p2-10-g1317: Оно is a neuter pronoun 'it' (referring back to the notion just mentioned). Встречается is present tense, imperfective, reflexive/intransitive 'is encountered / is found.' У takes the genitive (славянофилов) and here means 'among' or 'with'; А. is an abbreviated author initial or reference marker.
segments.p2-10-g1318: The single letter 'С.' here is an abbreviation used in citations; in this bibliography-like context it functions as a bibliographic marker for page(s). Grammatically it's not a word with morphological inflection but an abbreviated label.
segments.p2-10-g1349: A standalone numeral serving as a section or item number; written with a period as typographic punctuation. There is no verb or grammatical structure — it functions as a label/heading.
segments.p2-10-g1350: An abbreviated bibliographic marker (capital letter with a trailing period). Here 'С.' is an editorial abbreviation meaning 'страница/страницы' (page/pages); it is not inflected and stands alone as a reference label.
segments.p2-10-g1351: An elliptical bibliographic phrase: the cardinal number '178' names a page, followed by the coordinating conjunction 'и' ('and') which links it to the following abbreviated noun. The whole segment functions as a terse reference ('page 178 and notes').
segments.p2-10-g1352: A semicolon used to separate items in a list of references; it has no lexical content but marks a pause/division between reference fragments.
segments.p2-10-g1353: Abbreviation 'см.' (from 'смотри') used as an imperative-like bibliographic pointer meaning 'see' or 'cf.' It is a standard citation marker directing the reader to consult another place.
segments.p2-10-g1305: The numeral "1" here functions as a terse label (a chapter number) rather than as part of a running sentence; the following comma is a simple separator used in bibliographic or list-like notation.
segments.p2-10-g1306: "гл." is the standard abbreviation of глава ("chapter"). The period marks the abbreviation; it stands in the nominative singular as a label and is not inflected here. Placed after the number, it forms a compact citation-like phrase meaning "chapter 1."
segments.p2-10-g1307: The numeral "8" is another list or reference label; the semicolon after it separates this item from the next in a compact, punctuation-heavy citation style.
segments.p2-10-g1308: "ч." is the abbreviation of часть ("part"). The period indicates it's an abbreviation; used after a numeral it labels the larger division (part) that the number refers to. In this context it should be read as "part."
segments.p2-10-g1309: The numeral "2" serves as a brief label (here, a chapter number) and the comma acts as a separator in the compact reference notation.
segments.p2-10-g1310: As above, "гл." is the abbreviation for глава ("chapter"). The period marks the abbreviation; it stands in nominative label form and combines with the preceding number to mean "chapter 2."
segments.p2-10-g1311: The token "7)" (followed by a closing punctuation) is the end of a parenthetical or enumerated item: the numeral inside parentheses labels the item and the closing parenthesis (plus the following full stop in the surrounding text) marks the end of that parenthetical remark or list entry.
segments.p2-10-g1354: также is an adverb meaning “also” or “in addition”; here it stands alone and is followed by a colon to introduce a cross-reference or additional bibliographic information. Its position before the colon simply marks that what follows is an additional item.
segments.p2-10-g1355: Т. is an abbreviation (т.) for том (volume) and is written with a capital letter and a period in bibliographic contexts; it names the type of reference being introduced by 'также:'.
segments.p2-10-g1356: A numeral used as an item or volume number; the following period marks it as an ordinal/label in a bibliographic reference rather than a plain number in running text.
segments.p2-10-g1357: С. is an abbreviation (с.) for страница/страницы (page/pages) used in references; capitalized with a period, it precedes the page range that follows.
segments.p2-10-g1358: A page-range given as 285–287; the source uses a double hyphen to represent an en dash, and the closing parenthesis indicates this range is the end of a parenthetical reference.
segments.p2-10-g1344: соч. is an obvious bibliographic abbreviation of сочинения (or соч.), used here as a standalone noun in nominative form meaning “(collected) works.” It has no verb and functions as a short label in a citation.
segments.p2-10-g1345: Л. is an abbreviation (capitalized) for лист (leaf/page) in bibliographic contexts; it appears as a single-label element indicating a page/leaf reference. As an isolated abbreviation it carries no inflection or verb agreement.
segments.p2-10-g1346: A comma serving as the standard separator in a bibliographic entry, linking the preceding element (place or label) to the date.
segments.p2-10-g1347: 1975. is the year of publication written as an Arabic numeral with a trailing period (full stop) as commonly printed in citations; it functions as a date element and carries no grammatical inflection.
segments.p2-10-g1348: Т. is an abbreviation of том (volume) used in bibliographic citations to label a volume number; here it stands alone as a header-like element and is not inflected.
segments.p2-10-g1377: М. is the standard bibliographic abbreviation for Москва (Moscow); the final period marks it as an abbreviation and it stands as the place-of-publication element in the citation.
segments.p2-10-g1378: A comma used here as punctuation to separate the place-of-publication from the year in a bibliographic entry.
segments.p2-10-g1379: The four-digit numeral is the year of publication; the trailing period ends the bibliographic fragment.
segments.p2-10-g1380: С. is the abbreviation of страница/страницы (page/pages) used before page numbers in citations; the capital letter plus period marks it as an abbreviation introducing the page list.
segments.p2-10-g1381: The first page number (90) followed by a comma to separate multiple page references.
segments.p2-10-g1382: The second page number (116) followed by a period that terminates the bibliographic entry.
segments.p2-10-g1373: С. is the common bibliographic abbreviation of страница/страницы (page/pages). Here it appears alone and functions as the label that introduces a page reference; it links directly to the following segment that gives the page numbers.
segments.p2-10-g1374: A simple numeric range indicating the pages referenced (112–116). In bibliographic style this follows the abbreviation for pages (С.) and is read as a closed page interval.
segments.p2-10-g1375: This is a bibliographic list entry: the initial digit 2 marks the entry number; the surname Скафтымов appears first (standard Russian bibliographic order), followed by the abbreviated given name indicated by A. (period shows an initial). The pieces together form the author citation for item number 2.
segments.p2-10-g1376: This is a noun phrase functioning as a title: Нравственные (adjective, plural nominative) modifies искания (plural noun, nominative) and русских писателей is a genitive plural phrase ('of Russian writers') specifying whose searches these are. Word order is typical for Russian attributive phrases: adjective + noun + genitive attributive.
segments.p2-10-g1383: This is an item number plus a personal name: the initial numeral '3' marks a list entry. 'Назиров' is a surname in nominative form; placing the surname before the initial is a common bibliographic style. 'Р.' is a single-letter abbreviation with a trailing period, here functioning as the person's given-name initial (an abbreviation), not as a grammatical ending.
segments.p2-10-g1384: A single capital letter with a period: in isolation this represents an initial or other abbreviation. The form 'Г.' is an abbreviated form (period signals contraction); without context it’s ambiguous whether it stands for a name, place, or the word 'год/город', but here it functions as an initial/abbreviation rather than the listed lexical senses.
segments.p2-10-g1385: 'Творческие' is an adjective (nominative plural) placed before the noun it modifies. 'принципы' is a plural noun in the nominative forming the head of this noun phrase (a title or heading). 'Ф.' is a single-letter abbreviation (period included) representing an author's initial; it stands in apposition to the noun phrase (understood as 'of F.'), and because it's an abbreviation it does not show a separate case ending.
segments.p2-10-g1386: Another single-letter abbreviation with a period, here functioning as an initial (e.g. second initial in an author’s initials). The form 'М.' is written like an abbreviation rather than the grammatical label listed in the lemma; context shows it's an initial rather than the grammatical sense.
segments.p2-10-g1387: Достоевского is the surname достоевский in the genitive singular (ending -ого). In bibliographic or short-form contexts the genitive can indicate authorship or possession (literally “of Dostoevsky”); here it appears as a standalone bibliographic element and ends the sentence with a period.
segments.p2-10-g1388: Саратов is a proper noun in the nominative used as a place name (city), capitalized and followed by a comma to separate the place from the year in a citation.
segments.p2-10-g1389: 1982 is a four-digit numeral giving the year; the period is sentence-final punctuation. In citations the year follows the place separated by a comma.
segments.p2-10-g1390: С. is an abbreviation (с. or С.) for страница/страницы meaning “page(s).” It functions as the label that precedes page numbers in bibliographic entries; the period is part of the abbreviation.
segments.p2-10-g1391: 60-61 is a numeric page range using a hyphen to indicate inclusive pages; the final period ends the citation. This range pairs with the preceding abbreviation for pages.
segments.p2-10-g1325: Киреевского is the masculine proper name Кireevsky in the genitive singular (ending -ого). Here it appears as part of an authors' list in the genitive (i.e., 'in the works/of Kireevsky'); the trailing comma shows it is one item of enumeration.
segments.p2-10-g1326: а также is an idiomatic conjunction meaning 'and also' or 'as well'; у + genitive (Тургенева, Герцена) indicates 'in/with the works of' or 'among' those authors. И simply links the two names. Word order: conjunction + preposition + two genitive names.
segments.p2-10-g1327: О том is a topical prepositional phrase meaning 'about that' or 'as to that' (о + demonstrative том in the prepositional case). It introduces the subject matter of the following subordinate clause.
segments.p2-10-g1328: какой смысл вкладывал в это определение Достоевский is a subordinate clause meaning 'what meaning Dostoevsky put into this definition.' вкладывал is the imperfective past tense (3rd person singular) of вкладывать, implying a past/inherent action 'put/invested'; в + accusative это + определение = 'into this definition.' The subject Достоевский follows the verb, a normal Russian word-order variation.
segments.p2-10-g1329: в какой-то степени можно судить по словам Версилова в "Подростке" (1875) is the main clause: в какой-то степени ('to some degree') uses the indefinite construction какой-то (какой + particle то) meaning 'some/to some extent'; можно судить is an impersonal construction 'one can judge' or 'it is possible to judge'; по словам = 'by/according to the words (utterances) of' uses по + dative (словам).
segments.p2-10-g1330: Он говорит is simple present (or literary present used in narration) 3rd-person singular: 'he says' or 'he is saying.' It introduces direct speech; the pronoun Он is the subject and говорит is the verb 'to say/speak.'
segments.p2-10-g1331: This segment contains only the opening quotation mark in the source. Grammatically it marks the start of direct speech and contains no lexical items to analyze.
segments.p2-10-g1334: живая жизнь is a noun phrase (adjective + noun) in the nominative: 'living life' or 'real life' (живой used here as 'living/real, immediate'). It serves as the topic introduced by the quote.
segments.p2-10-g1335: то есть is a parenthetical equivalence 'that is.' The adjectives не умственная и не сочиненная are feminine nominative modifiers (agreeing with жизнь) meaning 'not intellectual and not invented/contrived.' Это должно быть is a neuter impersonal construction 'this must be/ought to be' (должно as modal), followed by нечто 'something' and the predicate ужасно простое 'terribly/extremely simple.' Note the sequence: explanation of what 'living life' should be, then the modal judgment.
segments.p2-10-g1336: самое обыденное и в глаза бросающееся is a further specification: самое ('the very') + обыденное ('ordinary/everyday') and a participial phrase в глаза бросающееся ('striking to the eye' literally 'throwing itself into the eyes'). в глаза is a set accusative phrase meaning 'to the eyes' and бросающееся is a present participle agreeing in gender/number with the neuter нечто implied earlier.
segments.p2-10-g1337: ежедневное и ежеминутное are two coordinating neuter adjectives ('daily' and 'every-minute') describing the same implied 'something' (нечто). The final punctuation closes the descriptive list.
segments.p2-10-g1363: "No" is the abbreviation for "number" (from Latin numero) placed before a numeral to indicate an issue or item; the period marks the abbreviation. This construction is invariant and simply states the ordinal/identifier of the item.
segments.p2-10-g1364: С. is an abbreviation used in bibliographic citations for "страница/страницы" (page/pages). It is capitalized and followed by a period; in English it corresponds to "p." (page) or "pp." (pages) and normally precedes the numeral(s) giving the page or page range.
segments.p2-10-g1365: The numerals "10--11" indicate an inclusive page range (the double hyphen here represents an en dash in print: 10–11). The closing parenthesis and period are part of the citation punctuation, ending the parenthetical reference.
segments.p2-10-g1366: Here "См." is the abbreviation of the verb "смотреть/смотрите" used in citations and footnotes to mean "see" or "compare." The preceding numeral "1" marks the footnote or reference number; the period marks the abbreviation. This is not the sense "centimeter."
segments.p2-10-g1359: Об + noun in the genitive (об источнике) means 'about/regarding the source'. Выражения is genitive ('of the expression') and thus modifies источник: 'source of the expression'. The quoted phrase "живая жизнь" names the expression; см. is the bibliographic abbreviation (imperative 'see') placed at the end to point the reader to the reference.
segments.p2-10-g1360: Статью is the accusative singular of статья ('article') and functions as the direct object of the implied 'see' from the previous line. В. is an initial (abbreviated given name); initials in Russian are not declined and typically precede the surname.
segments.p2-10-g1361: Н. is another initial (here the middle initial or second initial of the author's name). As an abbreviation it stands for a name element and is not inflected; it forms part of the author's initials.
segments.p2-10-g1362: Фойницкого is the genitive singular of the surname and here indicates the author of the article (the noun phrase стaтью + [initials] + Фойницкого = 'the article by ... Foynitsky'). В + prepositional (в журнале) means 'in the journal'; журнале is the prepositional singular of журнал. Русская речь is the title of the journal (kept as a proper name), and 1981 in parentheses gives the year of publication.
segments.p2-10-g1367: A colon punctuation mark introducing what follows; here it functions as part of the printed line (likely bibliographic or editorial) rather than a lexical item.
segments.p2-10-g1368: Белопольский is a capitalized surname placed before an initial in the common Russian bibliographic style (Surname + initial). В. is an abbreviated given name or patronymic initial (period marks the abbreviation); word order is surname then initial.
segments.p2-10-g1369: A single-letter uppercase initial with a period, standing for a personal name (given name or patronymic) abbreviated; functions as an initial rather than a full lexical word.
segments.p2-10-g1370: A nominal/title-like clause with no verb. Достоевский is a proper name in the nominative. И is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the name to the noun phrase that follows. Философская is an adjective modifying мысль ('philosophical thought'); его is the possessive pronoun 'his' referring back to Dostoevsky; эпохи is the genitive singular of 'эпоха' and completes the possessive relation ('thought of his era').
segments.p2-10-g1371: A fixed toponym composed of Rostov + the preposition-на + the noun Дон in the prepositional case (Дону): Ростов-на-Дону = 'Rostov-on-Don'. Here 'на' is the preposition 'on' as part of the name and 'Дону' is the prepositional singular form of 'Дон'. The trailing comma separates the place name from the year.
segments.p2-10-g1372: Arabic numerals giving the year (publication or printing date); the period marks the end of the sentence.

sentences.p2-10-s17: Even in my underground dreams I could not picture love any other way than as a struggle; I always began it with hatred and ended it by morally subduing (the beloved), and afterwards I could no longer even imagine what to do with the conquered object.

sentences.p2-10-s38: Then he immediately turned away and sprang as quickly as he could into another corner, so as not to see, at least.
